Year groups · Revision · Sources

📅 Year 7 — Medieval England 1066–1500

The Norman Conquest, medieval society, health and medicine, and castles.

📅⭐🌍🤖
📘 🔍 Inquiry📘 📝 Written Analysis📘 🧠 Metacognitive Thinking🔬 Analysis💡 Intellectual Curiosity🔗 Making Connections🎯 Self-awarenessSDG 4: Quality EducationSDG 16: Peace & Justice🔍 AI Critical Thinking⚖️ AI Ethics

⏱️ Chronology — History's First Tool

Imagine watching a film where the scenes are completely out of order. That is what history looks like without chronology — the ability to place events in the correct sequence across time. Historians use two systems: BC (Before Christ) and AD (Anno Domini — "in the year of the Lord"). The crucial rule that trips everyone up: BC counts backwards. The year 500 BC is 400 years older than 100 BC.

🎯 Activity 1: Crack the Chronology Code

📜 Primary vs Secondary Sources

📜 Primary Sources

Created at the time of the event — by someone who was there or had direct access to it.

Examples: The Bayeux Tapestry (c.1077) · A WW1 soldier's diary · A Tudor painting · A medieval law · A photograph taken at the scene
📚 Secondary Sources

Created after the event — using primary sources to draw conclusions. The person was not there.

Examples: Your history textbook · A 2024 documentary · An academic book · This website · A museum display

⚠️ Critical insight: A primary source is NOT automatically more reliable than a secondary one. An eyewitness can be biased, mistaken, or deliberately dishonest. A secondary source written by a careful historian may be far more accurate than a biased primary one. This is why we always apply WACA.

🎯 Activity 2: Source Surgery
  • Classify each source below as Primary or Secondary, then justify your decision in one sentence:
  • (a) The Bayeux Tapestry, c.1077   (b) Simon Schama's 'A History of Britain', 2000   (c) A WW1 soldier's photograph   (d) Spielberg's film 'Schindler's List', 1993   (e) A letter written by a medieval monk   (f) A Wikipedia article about the Black Death
  • For ONE primary source above, write a full WACA analysis including your final judgement sentence.
  • 💎 Extension: Can a work of fiction ever be a useful historical source? Argue both sides with specific examples.

🔍 The WACA Framework — Unlocking Every Source

Before using any source as evidence, interrogate it. WACA gives you four questions that reveal a source's true value — and its hidden limitations.

W
WHO?
Who created this source? What was their role, background, and possible reasons for bias?
A
AUDIENCE?
Who was this made for? A private diary is very different from a public speech or propaganda poster.
C
CONTEXT?
When was it created? What was happening at that time that might have shaped what was included or left out?
A
AIM?
What was the creator trying to achieve? To persuade? Celebrate? Record for posterity? Cover something up?
✍️ The WACA Judgement — Write It Like This
"This source is useful for understanding [WHAT] because [WACA evidence]. However, it is limited because [counter-WACA point]."

Key principle: A biased source is NOT useless. Propaganda is valuable evidence of what someone WANTED people to believe.

🎯 Activity 3: WACA Challenge
  • Apply full WACA to the Bayeux Tapestry (commissioned by Bishop Odo — William's half-brother — in the 1070s). Remember: Odo commissioned it, not a neutral historian.
  • Apply WACA to: (a) a WW1 recruitment poster from 1914; (b) a photograph of the D-Day landings taken by a soldier; (c) a modern history textbook chapter on WW1.
  • Write a WACA judgement sentence for each one.
  • 💎 Platinum: Compare your WACA analysis of the Bayeux Tapestry with how a 21st-century historian writes about Hastings. What has changed in how we understand this event — and why?

⚖️ Fact, Opinion and Cause & Effect

Three more tools every historian needs before they can write about the past effectively.

✅
Historical FACT
Something that can be proven — it either happened or it didn't. Example: "William defeated Harold at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066." This can be verified from multiple independent sources.
💭
Historical OPINION
A judgement or interpretation that cannot be proven beyond doubt. Example: "William was a better king than Harold would have been." Different historians may reach different conclusions from the same evidence.
⚡
CAUSE
What made an event happen. Causes can be long-term (building up over years) or short-term (the immediate trigger). Example: Harold's exhausted army = a CAUSE of his defeat at Hastings.
🌊
EFFECT
What happened as a result of an event. Effects can be immediate or long-term. Example: The Norman language transforming English = a long-term EFFECT of the Conquest.
🎯 Activity 4: Fact, Opinion or Cause/Effect?
  • Classify each statement below as FACT, OPINION, CAUSE, or EFFECT — and explain your reasoning in one sentence:
  • (a) "The Black Death arrived in England in June 1348."
  • (b) "The Black Death was the most devastating event in medieval history."
  • (c) "After the Black Death, fewer workers were available, so peasants could demand higher wages."
  • (d) "The Norman Conquest improved England by bringing new ideas from France."
  • (e) "The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was partly caused by the Black Death."
  • 💎 Challenge: Write your own example of each of the four categories, using events you have studied.

🏛️ Why History Matters

The skills you develop in history — WACA, chronology, distinguishing fact from opinion, identifying causes and effects — are not just useful in exams. They are the skills of critical thinking: the ability to interrogate a source, question an argument, and reach a justified conclusion. In a world of social media, fake news, and political spin, these might be the most important skills you ever learn.

📜 E.H. Carr, 'What is History?', 1961
"History is an unending dialogue between the present and the past."

The historian E.H. Carr argued that history is always shaped by the time in which the historian lives. What does this mean for how we should read history books?

🎯 Activity 5: The History Dinner Party
🎯 Activity 1: Crack the Chronology Code
  • Place these in order oldest first: AD 1066 · 55 BC · AD 1348 · 776 BC · AD 43 · AD 1807 · 10,000 BC · AD 1215
  • Create an annotated timeline from 55 BC (Caesar invades Britain) to AD 1485 (Tudor period begins). Include at least 8 events — write one sentence explaining each event's significance.
  • Explain to someone younger: why does BC count backwards? What analogy helps?
🎯 Activity 2: Primary or Secondary? Source Surgery
  • Classify each as Primary or Secondary AND give a one-sentence justification: (a) The Bayeux Tapestry, c.1077; (b) Simon Schama's 'A History of Britain', 2000; (c) A WW1 soldier's photograph; (d) Spielberg's film 'Schindler's List', 1993; (e) A letter written by a medieval monk
  • For ONE primary source above, write a full WACA analysis with your final judgement sentence.
  • Extension: Can a work of fiction ever be a useful historical source? Argue both sides.
🎯 Activity 3: WACA Challenge — The Bayeux Tapestry
  • The Bayeux Tapestry was commissioned by Bishop Odo — William's half-brother — in the 1070s. Apply full WACA: Who? Audience? Context? Aim?
  • Does knowing Odo commissioned it make the Tapestry MORE or LESS useful as evidence of Hastings?
  • Write your complete WACA judgement sentence for the Bayeux Tapestry.
  • Compare: apply WACA to a modern news article about a political event. What similarities do you find in how bias works across different time periods?
🎯 Activity 4: Fact, Opinion, Cause or Effect?
  • Classify each statement AND justify: (a) 'The Black Death arrived in England in June 1348.'; (b) 'The Black Death was the most devastating event in medieval history.'; (c) 'After the Black Death, fewer workers meant peasants could demand higher wages.'; (d) 'The Norman Conquest improved England.'; (e) 'The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was partly caused by the Black Death.'
  • Write your own example of each of the four categories using events you have studied.
🎯 Activity 5: The History Dinner Party
  • Invite THREE people from history to dinner. For each: who they are, why you chose them, and the ONE question you would ask.
  • Apply WACA to their potential answers: why might each guest's account be unreliable — even though they were there?
  • Write a 200-word conversation between two guests who completely disagree about an event they both witnessed. Show how the same events look different from different perspectives.
  • Invite THREE people from history to dinner. For each: explain who they are, why you chose them, and the ONE question you would ask.
  • Apply WACA to their potential answers: why might each guest's account be unreliable — even if they were there?
  • 💎 Platinum: Write a 300-word conversation between two of your guests who completely disagree about an event they both witnessed. Show how the same event looks different from different perspectives.
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “History is not what happened. It is what historians can find evidence for, argue for, and persuade others to believe.” — E.H. Carr, 1961

📘 🔍 Inquiry📘 📝 Written Analysis📘 🔍 Source Evaluation🔬 Analysis💡 Intellectual Curiosity🔗 Making Connections🎯 Self-awarenessSDG 4: Quality EducationSDG 16: Peace & Justice🔍 AI Critical Thinking

👑 England in January 1066 — A Kingdom Without an Heir

📷 A map of England in 1066 showing the main earldoms and settlements.

On 5 January 1066, King Edward the Confessor died without a clear successor. In that single moment, he triggered the most dramatic year in English history. Three men immediately claimed the throne — each with a different argument for why the crown was rightfully theirs. Within nine months, England would fight three battles, bury two kings, and begin a transformation that would last for centuries.

📊 England in 1065 — Key Facts
  • 👥 Population: approximately 1.5 million people
  • 🌾 Most people were farmers living in small villages
  • 💰 Wealthy country — fertile land, wool trade, silver mines
  • ⚔️ Run by a king supported by powerful earls
  • 🤴 King Edward had no children — a succession crisis was inevitable
  • 📅 Edward had spent years in Normandy — bringing Norman friends and advisors
⚠️ Why the Succession Crisis Mattered

When a medieval king died without a clear heir, powerful men had to decide who ruled next. The Witan (royal council) had the power to choose — but so did armies. In 1066, three very different men all believed the throne should be theirs. Their competing claims would be settled not by law, but by battle.

👑 Three Men, One Crown — The Claimants

📷 The three claimants to the English throne in 1066: Harold Godwinson, Harald Hardrada and William of Normandy.

Each claimant had a different type of argument. Harold had support and presence. Hardrada had a legal treaty. William had a promise AND an oath. Understanding each claim is essential for explaining why 1066 happened.

🏴 Harold Godwinson

England's most powerful earl. Claimed Edward named him on his deathbed. Crowned the very day after Edward died — before any rival could react.

✅ Already in England with strong support.
❌ No royal blood — technically seized the crown.
🛡️ Harald Hardrada

King of Norway. Based his claim on an old Viking treaty. Nicknamed 'the Hard Ruler' — most feared warrior in Europe. Huge Viking army.

✅ Enormous battle-hardened army.
❌ Very remote claim through an old treaty.
🏰 William of Normandy

Duke of Normandy. Claimed Edward promised him the throne in 1051. Also claimed Harold had sworn an oath on holy relics to support his claim.

✅ Papal blessing + Harold's oath made Harold look like a oath-breaker.
❌ Had to invade across the sea — a huge military risk.
🎯 Activity 1: Three Claimants Investigation
  • Create a detailed fact-file for each of the three claimants. For each include: their claim, main strength, main weakness, and what evidence supports their case.
  • Rank the three claimants by strength of claim. Justify each ranking with specific evidence.
  • Who had the strongest LEGAL claim? Who had the strongest MILITARY claim? Were they the same person?
  • 'Harold Godwinson had no legitimate claim to the throne in 1066.' How far do you agree? Write an 8-mark answer.

⚔️ Three Battles, One Year — 1066

📷 The Battle of Hastings, 14 October 1066. Apply WACA to this image: who produced it, for whom, in what context?
20 SepBattle of Gate Fulford: Harald Hardrada defeats the northern earls Edwin and Morcar. Yorkshire is in Viking hands.
25 SepBattle of Stamford Bridge: Harold rushes north and defeats Hardrada decisively. Hardrada killed. Harold needs only 24 ships to take survivors home. Then a messenger arrives from the south...
28 SepWilliam lands at Pevensey Bay in Sussex with 7,000 men. Harold hears the news at Stamford Bridge and must march 250 miles south immediately.
14 OctBattle of Hastings: Harold's exhausted army faces William's cavalry and archers on Senlac Hill. Harold killed late in the afternoon. England has a new king.
25 DecWilliam crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey — but it took two months to march there, garrison towns, and accept English surrender.
📜 The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle — on the Battle of Hastings, c.1100
"Harold was killed, and Leofwine his brother, and Earl Gyrth his brother, and many good men. And the Frenchmen had possession of the place of slaughter, all as God granted them because of the people's sins."

Apply WACA: Written by English monks about 34 years after the battle. Note the phrase 'because of the people's sins' — what does this tell us about how the chroniclers explained defeat? Who? Audience? Context? Aim?

🎯 Activity 2: Why Did Harold Lose at Hastings?
  • Historians suggest many reasons for Harold's defeat. Evaluate each reason by ranking it 1 (most important) to 5 (least important), then justify your ranking:
  • (a) Harold's army was exhausted after Stamford Bridge and the march south; (b) William's cavalry and archers were superior; (c) The fake retreat trick broke the shield wall; (d) Harold was unlucky — possibly hit by an arrow; (e) Harold rushed south without waiting for fresh troops
  • Apply WACA to the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle source above. Write your full judgement sentence.
  • 'Harold's own mistakes were more important than William's strengths in deciding the outcome of Hastings.' How far do you agree?

🗺️ After Hastings — William's Road to the Crown

📷 William the Conqueror's coronation at Westminster Abbey, 25 December 1066.

Winning at Hastings was just the beginning. William had defeated Harold's army, but England had not surrendered. He needed to march to London, accept the submission of the remaining English leaders, and be crowned king — all while making clear that resistance would be crushed. The route he took tells us a great deal about how the Norman Conquest actually worked.

📜 The account of William's march from Hastings, from the document 'Following the Battle of Hastings'
"Following the battle of Hastings, it took over two months for William to be crowned King of England. William marched through Dover, Canterbury, and around London before the English leaders submitted at Wallingford and Berkhamsted. He was crowned at Westminster Abbey on Christmas Day 1066."

This route was deliberate — William was demonstrating his power, garrisoning key towns, and forcing submission before his coronation. Consider: why did William go AROUND London rather than through it?

🎯 Activity 3: William's March — Map Work and Analysis
  • Task 1 — Map Work: Using an outline map of southern England, mark the route William took from Hastings to Westminster Abbey on Christmas Day 1066. Include: Hastings, Dover, Canterbury, London, Wallingford, Berkhamsted, Westminster. Add a brief note at each location explaining what happened there.
  • Task 2 — Analysis: Why did William go AROUND London rather than directly through it? What does this tell us about his military thinking?
  • Task 3 — Creative Writing: Imagine you are a soldier in William's army. Write a diary entry covering: your arrival in England, the march through southern England, and being present at the coronation on Christmas Day 1066. Stick to the historical facts but add vivid descriptive detail. Include: what you saw, how people reacted, how you felt.

🏰 The Long-Term Consequences of 1066

📷 A page from the Domesday Book (1086) — William's survey of all land and wealth in England.

William's victory at Hastings on 14 October 1066 began a transformation of England that affected everything: language, law, buildings, the Church, and national identity. The changes were so profound that historians still debate their full impact today.

🗺️
The Domesday Book (1086)
William surveyed everything England owned — every farm, mill, and ox recorded. One of the most remarkable administrative documents in history. It was called Domesday because there was no appeal against it.
⚔️
The Feudal System
All land ultimately belonged to the king, given to barons for military service. Barons gave land to knights. Knights kept the peace. Peasants worked the land. England's society was rebuilt from scratch.
🗣️
Language
French became the language of the ruling class. English survived only among the peasantry. After 1066, English absorbed thousands of French words: justice, parliament, castle, army, court, beauty.
⛪
The Norman Church
William replaced almost every English bishop and abbot with Norman ones. New stone cathedrals and abbeys built across England — Durham, Canterbury, Lincoln — transforming the landscape.
🎯 Activity 4: The Long-Term Impact of 1066
  • Choose ONE consequence of the Norman Conquest from the grid above. Research it in depth and write 150 words explaining: (a) what changed; (b) why it was significant; (c) how long the change lasted.
  • Apply WACA to the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle source. Write your judgement sentence.
  • 'The Norman Conquest of 1066 was the most important event in English history.' Using the material above, write a 16-mark essay arguing both for and against this claim. Use PEEL structure throughout.
🎥 Dan Snow · The History Guy · Crash Course
🎥 Dan Snow: The Norman Conquest — Why It Changed Everything🎥 History Guy: The Battle of Hastings 1066🎥 Crash Course: The Dark Ages and the Norman Conquest
📚 Explore in Depth
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “The Norman Conquest was the greatest catastrophe that ever happened to England — an exchange of one ruling class for another that spoke a completely different language.” — Marc Morris, 2012
💡 Did You Know?
Harold marched 250 miles from Stamford Bridge to Hastings in just 13 days.

📅⭐🌍🤖
📘 📝 Written Analysis📘 🔍 Source Evaluation📘 💬 Debate🔬 Analysis⚖️ Evaluation🔗 Making Connections💪 ResilienceSDG 4: Quality EducationSDG 16: Peace & Justice🔍 AI Critical Thinking

🎉 What Was a Holy Day?

The word "holiday" comes directly from "holy day" — and that single fact reveals everything about medieval leisure. Ordinary people had almost no free time. They worked from dawn to dusk for their lord, six or seven days a week. But on religious feast days — around 50 per year — they were briefly free. In those precious hours of freedom, medieval entertainment came to life.

🎯 Activity 1: How Much Free Time?

⚽ Mob Football — Banned Repeatedly, Played Regardless

Imagine football with no rules, no referee, no fixed team size, and goals sometimes several miles apart. That was mob football — and multiple kings banned it. People played it anyway, which itself tells us something important about the relationship between ordinary people and royal authority.

📋 The (Non-) Rules
  • ⚽ Ball: an inflated pig's bladder
  • 👥 Players: entire villages — sometimes hundreds on each side
  • 🗺️ Goals: could be miles apart in different villages
  • ⚖️ Rules: almost none — "anything short of murder"
  • 💀 Injuries and occasionally deaths were not uncommon
  • 📅 Usually played on Shrove Tuesday (pancake day)
🚫 Royal Bans — And Why They Failed
1314Edward II bans football in London — completely ignored
1331Edward III bans it — still ignored
1363Edward III bans it AGAIN — wants men practising archery
1667Still being banned. Still being played.
📜 Source A — William Fitzstephen, monk, c.1174 (first written description of football)
"After dinner, all the youth of the city go out into the fields to take part in the ball game. The students of each school have their own ball; the workers from each city craft are also carrying their balls."

Apply WACA: Fitzstephen was a Norman monk writing about London. What might he have left out about how violent the game was? What does 'youth of the city' suggest about who could participate?

📜 Source B — A royal proclamation banning football, 14th century
"Because of the great uproar in the city caused by the hustling over large balls, from which many evils might arise, we do forbid this game henceforth in the city."

Apply WACA: who issued this? Why do you think it uses the phrase 'many evils might arise'? What were the kings actually worried about?

🎯 Activity 2: The Football Bans Debate
  • Using Sources A and B: write two paragraphs arguing about whether football should have been banned: one FROM the king's perspective (use Source B as your evidence) and one FROM an ordinary peasant's perspective (use Source A as your starting point).
  • Apply WACA to both sources. Which is more reliable? Which is more useful?
  • Why did the bans consistently fail for over 350 years? What does this tell us about royal power over ordinary people's daily lives?
  • 🥇 Gold: 'The football bans reveal that medieval kings had far less control over ordinary people than historians once suggested.' How far do you agree? Use both sources as evidence. 💎 Platinum: Write an 8-mark source evaluation comparing the usefulness of Sources A and B for understanding medieval leisure.

🎵 Music, Minstrels, and Miracle Plays

Before recorded music, radio, or television, entertainment was entirely live — and the wandering minstrel was medieval England's answer to a streaming service. These professional performers travelled from castle to village to market town, earning their keep with songs, stories, news, acrobatics, and magic tricks. They were simultaneously entertainers, news anchors, and the social media of their age.

🎭 Kinds of Entertainment
  • 🎵 Minstrels — professional musicians telling stories in song
  • 🤹 Jugglers & acrobats — at fairs and on market days
  • 🎪 Mystery plays — religious dramas performed in town streets
  • 🐻 Bear-baiting — brutal but extremely popular with all classes
  • 🎲 Dice, chess, cards — for merchants, nobles, and clergy
  • 🏹 Archery contests — civic duty AND sport; kings encouraged them
🏆 Sports & Physical Games
  • ⛸️ Ice skating — on animal bones strapped to boots
  • 🎯 Bowls, quoits, stool-ball — forerunners of cricket and golf
  • 🐓 Cock-fighting — widespread; heavily gambled upon
  • 🐟 Fishing & hunting — hunting strictly reserved for the nobility
  • 🏇 Jousting tournaments — spectacular events for the knightly class
  • 🌰 Conkers — yes, medieval children played conkers too
🎯 Activity 3: Rich vs Poor Entertainment
  • Create a table comparing the entertainment available to: (a) a wealthy noble; (b) a merchant in a market town; (c) a peasant in a rural village. For each class, include at least 4 specific activities.
  • Which activities were shared across all classes — and which were restricted to only one class? What does this tell us about medieval society?
  • Why did kings want their knights to practice archery rather than play football? Use Source B above to help you answer.
  • 💎 Platinum: 'Medieval entertainment was a direct reflection of the rigid class structure of medieval society.' How far do you agree? Use specific evidence from the activities you have studied.

👗 Fashion — What Your Clothes Said About You

In medieval England, your clothing was your identity badge. Everyone could tell at a glance exactly who you were, how wealthy you were, and where you stood in society — just by looking at what you wore. "Sumptuary laws" tried to prevent people from dressing above their station. Peasants in silk? Illegal. Merchants wearing fur? Technically a crime. And yet the laws were repeatedly broken — which itself tells us something very important.

👑
Royalty & High Nobility
Purple, ermine fur, silk brocade, gold thread, precious jewels. Purple dye cost more than gold per gram — it had to be imported from across the Mediterranean world.
⚔️
Knights & Gentry
Fine English wools, leather, decorated sword belts, quality boots. Limited fur trim. Bright colours from good English dyes. Clearly superior to peasant dress.
🌾
Peasants & Serfs
Rough undyed wool, coarse linen. Browns, greys, muddy greens — the natural colours of cheap, locally available dyes. No silk. No fur. No jewels. No colour.
🎯 Activity 4: The Sumptuary Laws Investigation
  • Research and explain: what were sumptuary laws? Find at least THREE specific examples of things that peasants or merchants were forbidden to wear.
  • Why did Parliament keep passing sumptuary laws throughout the 14th and 15th centuries? And why did they keep failing?
  • Design two medieval outfits — one for a wealthy noble and one for a peasant. Label every element with: (a) the material; (b) whether it was legally permitted for this class; (c) what it reveals about the wearer's wealth and status.
  • 💎 Platinum: 'The fact that sumptuary laws were repeatedly broken shows that medieval society was far more socially mobile than historians once believed.' How far do you agree? Use evidence from fashion AND food sections.

🍽️ Food — A Tale of Two Very Different Tables

👑 The Noble's Great Feast
  • 🦢 Roast swan, peacock, venison, wild boar
  • 🌶️ Exotic spices imported from Asia: pepper, cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg
  • 🍷 Imported wines from France and Spain — never water
  • 🎂 Elaborate sugar sculptures called "subtleties" displayed between courses
  • 🍖 6–12 courses — a great feast could last 4–5 hours
  • 🥄 Spices were status symbols — the more you used, the richer you were
🌾 The Peasant's Daily Bowl
  • 🍞 Rough brown bread — with grit that gradually wore teeth down to stumps
  • 🥣 Pottage (thick vegetable and grain stew) — eaten almost every day
  • 🧅 Onions, leeks, peas, beans — from the cottage garden
  • 🐟 Salted or dried fish on Fridays — Church rules required it
  • 🍺 Weak ale instead of water — water from rivers and wells was often unsafe
  • 🐭 Pest infestations in grain stores were a constant problem
🎯 Activity 5: A Feast for All Seasons?
  • Plan a medieval feast menu for a wealthy noble's winter celebration. Include: a minimum of 5 courses, the specific meats and spices used, the wines served, and a decorative "subtlety" for the grand finale. Explain the symbolic importance of each course.
  • Now plan what a peasant family might eat during that same week in winter. What challenges would they face: shortage of fresh vegetables, limited meat, risk of bread going mouldy?
  • Compare the two menus. Write a paragraph explaining what a medieval person's diet reveals about their social class, wealth, and access to trade networks.
  • 💎 Challenge: Some historians argue that peasant food was actually healthier than noble food because it had more vegetables and less rich meat. Do you agree — or were the disadvantages of peasant food (grit, contamination, monotony, shortage) worse?
🎯 Activity 1: How Much Free Time Did Medieval People Have?
  • Use specific evidence from what you have read above to support all your answers. Apply the WACA framework to any sources and use precise historical vocabulary.
  • Apply the WACA framework to any sources you use.
  • Share your response with the class and compare your conclusions.
🎯 Activity 2: The Great Football Debate
  • Using Sources A and B: write one paragraph FOR banning football (king's perspective using Source B) and one AGAINST (peasant's perspective using Source A).
  • Apply WACA to both sources. Which is more reliable? Which is more useful?
  • 🥇 'The football bans reveal medieval kings had far less control over ordinary people than historians suggest.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark source evaluation comparing Sources A and B.
🎯 Activity 3: Rich vs Poor Entertainment
  • Create a comparison table: entertainment for (a) a wealthy noble, (b) a merchant, (c) a peasant. At least 4 specific activities per class.
  • Which activities were shared? Which were restricted? What does this reveal about medieval society?
  • 💎 'Medieval entertainment was a direct reflection of the rigid class structure of medieval society.' How far do you agree?
🎯 Activity 4: Fashion and the Sumptuary Laws
  • Design two medieval outfits — one for a noble, one for a peasant. Label each element: material, whether it was legally permitted, what it reveals about status.
  • Research sumptuary laws: what were they? Why did Parliament keep passing them? Why did they keep failing?
  • 💎 'Sumptuary laws show that medieval society was more socially mobile than historians once believed.' How far do you agree?
🎯 Activity 5: A Feast for All Seasons?
  • Plan a medieval feast menu for a wealthy noble's winter celebration (at least 5 courses). Then plan what a peasant family might eat that same week.
  • Write a paragraph: what does a medieval person's diet reveal about their social class, wealth, and access to trade?
  • 💎 Some historians argue peasant food was actually healthier. Do you agree — or were the disadvantages worse?
⚡ Quick Quiz
Where does the modern English word "holiday" come from?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “Medieval people had a sophisticated culture — but access depended entirely on what class you were born into.” — Historian's perspective
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬

Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
What does medieval entertainment reveal about inequality and social control?

🗺️ How the Black Death Swept Across Europe

The Black Death did not arrive overnight — it followed the great trade routes of the medieval world westward from Central Asia like an unstoppable tide. By June 1348 it had reached the English port of Weymouth, Dorset, carried by fleas on the rats aboard merchant ships. Within two years, between a third and half of England's entire population was dead. Nothing in human history before or since has killed so many people so quickly.

📅 The Plague's Deadly Journey
1346Black Death reaches the Black Sea — carried along Silk Road trade routes from Central Asia
1347Reaches Sicily, Italy, and southern France — killing up to 50% of some city populations
Jun 1348Arrives at Weymouth, Dorset — via infected merchant ships from the continent
Late 1348Spreads rapidly north through England, reaching London by autumn
1349-50Scotland, Ireland, Scandinavia — virtually nowhere in Europe escapes
🎯 Activity 1: The Spread of the Black Death

🔬 Two Killers in One — Understanding the Disease

Medieval people did not know it, but the Black Death was actually two related diseases striking simultaneously — which is part of why it was so devastating. Even today, Yersinia pestis — the bacterium responsible — is considered one of the most dangerous pathogens ever to affect humans.

🐀 Bubonic Plague
  • 🦟 Carried in the stomach of fleas living on black rats
  • 🔴 Symptoms: painful buboes (swellings the size of an egg) in armpits, groin, and neck
  • ⚫ Black blotches appear as blood pools under the skin
  • 🌡️ Raging fever, vomiting, intense thirst, confusion
  • 💀 Killed 30–70% of those infected — usually within 3–5 days
💨 Pneumonic Plague
  • 💨 Spread through the air — by coughing and sneezing in crowded spaces
  • 🫁 Attacks the lungs directly, causing victims to cough up blood
  • 🤢 Breath becomes foul as lung tissue rots from within
  • 🔵 A bluish skin tint often appears (septicaemic variant)
  • 💀 Almost always fatal — death within just 1–3 days
🎯 Activity 2: Symptoms Investigation
  • Create a fact file for a medieval doctor treating Black Death patients in 1349. Include: the two types of plague, their key symptoms, how they spread, and why they are so difficult to treat without modern knowledge.
  • Why do you think having TWO types of plague made the outbreak so much worse than if there had been just one?
  • Compare the symptoms of Bubonic and Pneumonic plague. Which type do you think medieval people would have found MORE terrifying — and why?
  • 💎 Extension: Research how Yersinia pestis was finally identified as the cause (1894). Why did it take so long — and what does this tell us about the limits of medieval science?

🩺 Five Theories — All Wrong, But All Logical

Medieval medicine was built on ancient Greek ideas about four "humours" in the body — blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile. Disease meant these were out of balance. Not one of the five main theories about the Black Death was correct — but every single one made perfect logical sense given what people knew at the time. It is unfair to simply mock them.

✝️
God's Punishment
God was punishing humanity for its sins. The cure was prayer, fasting, confession, and sincere repentance. Churches received huge donations as people desperately sought divine protection.
🌿
Miasma — Bad Air
Poisonous vapours rising from rotting matter spread through the air and entered the body. Doctors carried herb-filled pomanders to block the smell. Streets were strewn with sweet-smelling flowers.
⭐
Planetary Alignment
Saturn, Jupiter, and Mars had aligned in 1345, poisoning the atmosphere. Astrologers became essential advisers. Nobody went outside without consulting their horoscope first.
👥
Scapegoating
False accusations that Jewish communities had poisoned water supplies. There was absolutely no evidence. Led to horrific violence across Germany and France.
🩸
Weak Humours
Cold, moist air upset the body's natural balance of humours, making it vulnerable. Doctors prescribed bloodletting (cutting a vein to release excess blood), purging, and avoiding baths.
📜 A 14th-century physician's manual (adapted)
"Flee the disease quickly, go far away, and return slowly. Avoid bathing — it opens the pores to the plague. If a bubo appears, place a live hen against the swelling to draw out the poison. Burn aromatic herbs in every room."

Apply WACA to this source. What does it tell us about medieval medicine — and what did it accidentally get right (isolation/quarantine) alongside what it completely got wrong?

🎯 Activity 3: Why Did People Believe Wrong Theories?
  • Choose TWO of the five theories above. For each: (a) explain why it seemed logical to a medieval person; (b) explain what evidence they might have used to support it; (c) explain what a modern scientist would say about it.
  • Why is it unfair to simply say "medieval people were stupid" about their theories? What does this tell us about the relationship between evidence and belief?
  • Apply WACA to the physician's manual above. Who wrote it? For whom? When? What was the aim? Does this make it useful evidence?
  • 🥇 Gold: 'Medieval doctors cannot be blamed for failing to treat the Black Death.' How far do you agree? 💎 Platinum: Write a 400-word essay arguing that medieval people were rational — they just had the wrong information.

❌ Cures That Didn't Work — But Why People Tried Them

🩸
Bloodletting
Cutting a vein to drain "excess blood" and rebalance the humours. The patient was already weakened by disease — this often made them worse or killed them directly.
🌹
Herb Pomanders
Bunches of herbs and flowers held to the nose to block the "bad air." Actually accidentally helpful — reduced flea exposure. But not enough to prevent infection.
🐔
Live Chicken Remedy
Shaving a chicken's bottom and pressing it against a bubo to "draw out the poison." Completely ineffective — and unpleasant for both patient and chicken.
🙏
Flagellants
Groups of people publicly whipping themselves, believing this would show God sufficient suffering to end the plague. Spread infection as they moved from town to town.
💊
Theriac
An ancient medicine made from vipers' flesh, honey, and 64 other ingredients. Sold as a universal cure. Completely useless against plague.
🎯 Activity 4: The Medieval Doctor's Dilemma
  • You are a medieval physician called to treat a wealthy merchant who has developed a bubo under his arm. Based on the knowledge available to you in 1349 (you believe in the four humours and miasma theory), describe exactly what you would do — step by step. Justify each decision using medieval medical theory.
  • Now write a response from a 21st-century microbiologist explaining what you should actually have done — and why the medieval approach was harmful.
  • What is one thing medieval doctors accidentally did that WAS helpful — even if for the wrong reasons?

⚡ The Causal Chain — From Catastrophe to Change

The Black Death didn't just kill people — it permanently shattered the social structure of medieval England. The consequences rippled outwards for generations, eventually bringing about changes that nobody in 1348 could possibly have predicted.

Black Death (1348–50) kills 30–50% of England's population → Labour shortage: far fewer workers available → survivors can demand higher wages → Lords resist: Parliament passes the Statute of Labourers (1351) — trying to cap wages at pre-plague levels → Peasants rebel: The Peasants' Revolt (1381) — thousands march on London → Long term: feudalism slowly collapses. Serfdom disappears. Peasants gain freedom.
🎯 Activity 5: The Causal Chain Challenge
🎯 Activity 1: Map the Spread of the Black Death
  • Using an outline map of Europe, trace the Black Death's route from Central Asia to Britain (1346–1350). Add dates and death toll estimates for each region.
  • Why did the plague travel along trade routes? What does this reveal about the advantages AND disadvantages of medieval international trade?
  • 'The Black Death was caused by the very thing that had made medieval Europe prosperous — international trade.' How far do you agree?
🎯 Activity 2: Doctor, Doctor!
  • You are a medieval physician in 1349. Write your official report to the King explaining: (a) what causes the plague — use the actual medieval theories; (b) three treatments you recommend; (c) advice for the population on how to stay safe.
  • Now write the same report from a 21st-century doctor's perspective. What do they know that a 14th-century physician couldn't?
  • Is it fair to mock medieval doctors? What were they actually working with?
  • Apply WACA to the physician's manual source above. Write your full judgement sentence.
🎯 Activity 3: Survivor's Diary
  • You survived the Black Death in a village that lost half its people. Write a diary entry describing: what you saw, how the village has changed, and what you believe caused it — using at least TWO of the medieval theories.
  • Research: find and apply WACA to Jean de Venette's actual 14th-century account of the Black Death. Compare it with your diary.
🎯 Activity 4: The Causal Chain Challenge
  • Draw the causal chain as a flowchart with at least 6 steps: Black Death → Labour shortage → Statute of Labourers → Peasants' Revolt → End of feudalism. For each arrow, write one sentence explaining WHY one event caused the next.
  • Which step was the most important turning point? Why?
  • 'The Black Death was the most important event in medieval English history.' Argue BOTH sides, then write a 16-mark PEEL essay reaching a justified conclusion.
🏛️ Historian's Verdict
“The Black Death was not just a catastrophe — it was the beginning of the end of feudalism. The survivors knew their value, and they demanded it.”— David Herlihy, historian
⚡ Quick Quiz
Which of these best describes how the Black Death spread in England from 1348?
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning
Hello! Let's examine the Black Death together. Here's a challenging question: medieval people were wrong about what caused the plague — but were their theories actually irrational, given what they knew? What do you think?
💬
Blog Spot
Argue · Evidence · Debate
Was the Black Death a disaster — or did it ultimately improve the lives of those who survived it?

📅⭐🌍🤖
📘 🔍 Inquiry📘 📝 Written Analysis📘 🔍 Source Evaluation🔬 Analysis💡 Intellectual Curiosity🔗 Making ConnectionsSDG 4: Quality EducationSDG 16: Peace & Justice🔍 AI Critical Thinking

🏗️ Why William Built Castles — Fast

After defeating Harold at Hastings, William faced an enormous problem: how do you permanently control a whole country whose people hate you? His answer was brilliant in its simplicity — stone, timber, and psychological terror. Within months of the Conquest, wooden fortresses were appearing across England. In Cambridge, 27 houses were demolished to make way for a castle. In Lincoln, over 160 homes were torn down. The message to the English was unmistakable: we are here to stay.

🎯 Activity 1: Where Would YOU Build a Castle?

🌲 Stage 1: The Motte-and-Bailey (1066–1100)

The genius of the motte-and-bailey design was its speed. A skilled team could complete one in as few as 8 days. Within 20 years of the Conquest, over 500 had appeared across England — an extraordinary achievement of military engineering.

🗺️
The MOTTE
A raised mound of earth — sometimes up to 10 metres high. Topped with a wooden tower where the lord lived and kept watch. The height gave defenders a massive tactical advantage: attackers had to climb while being shot at from above.
🏡
The BAILEY
An enclosed courtyard at the base of the motte, surrounded by a wooden palisade fence and a defensive ditch. Housed the stables, kitchens, soldiers' barracks, smithy, and chapel. Life in the bailey was busy and often noisy.
🔥
Critical Weakness: Fire
A single burning arrow on a dry day could destroy years of construction. Wood burns — and Norman England's enemies knew it. One successful fire attack could eliminate an entire castle in hours.
⛏️
Critical Weakness: Rot
Even without attack, English rain rotted the wooden structures within 20–30 years. The Normans needed a permanent solution — and they found it in stone.
🎯 Activity 2: Design a Motte-and-Bailey
  • Draw and label a motte-and-bailey castle from above (bird's eye view). Include: the motte, the keep, the bailey, the palisade, the ditch, the drawbridge, the gatehouse, living quarters, stables, and a well.
  • For each feature, write one sentence explaining its defensive purpose.
  • Which feature do you think was most important for defence — and why?
  • 💎 Challenge: Design an improved motte-and-bailey that solves the fire and rot problems without using stone. Is it possible?

🪨 Stage 2: The Stone Keep Castle (1070 onwards)

From around 1070, wealthier barons began replacing wood with stone. The results were magnificent and terrifying — walls 3–4 metres thick that could withstand battering rams, fire, and almost any weapon of the time. These were not just superior defences; they were permanent statements of Norman power, visible for miles across the flat English landscape.

🏰
The Keep
Central stone tower — the lord's home and last refuge. Walls up to 4 metres thick. Contained the great hall, private chambers, and a dark dungeon below ground level.
💧
The Moat
A wide, water-filled ditch surrounding the walls. Impossible to tunnel under without flooding. Made direct assault extremely dangerous.
🚪
The Gatehouse
The most heavily defended point — a drawbridge (that could be raised), a heavy portcullis (iron grille), thick wooden doors, and armed guards. The only way in.
🎯
Battlements
Raised sections of wall with gaps called crenellations. Defenders could step behind the raised parts for shelter, then lean out through the gaps to fire arrows.
🔥
Murder Holes
Openings in the ceiling of the gatehouse passage (called a mantrap). Defenders above could pour boiling water, burning oil, or heavy stones directly onto attackers below.
🌀
Spiral Stairs
Built clockwise when ascending. A right-handed defender coming DOWN the stairs had full sword-swinging room. An attacker going UP had their sword arm blocked by the central pillar. A deliberately lethal design.
📜 Norman chronicler describing the Tower of London, c.1100
"The tower, which they call the White Tower... is of such size and strength that it should be worthy of the name of the city itself."

Apply WACA: who wrote this? When? For whom? What was the aim? Does calling it 'worthy of the city' suggest military value, symbolic value, or both?

🎯 Activity 3: Stone Keep Investigation
  • Using the feature cards above, explain why stone keeps were superior to motte-and-bailey castles in every one of the following areas: defence against fire · defence against tunnelling · long-term durability · psychological impact.
  • Apply WACA to the Norman chronicler's description of the Tower of London. Is this a reliable source? What might have been exaggerated — and why?
  • 🥇 Gold: 'The Tower of London was more important as a symbol of power than as a military fortification.' Use the WACA source and your own knowledge to argue both sides. 💎 Platinum: Write a full 8-mark answer to this question using PEEL structure.

🐷 The Siege of Rochester, 1215 — 40 Fat Pigs Win a Battle

Castles were brilliant at defence. But how did attackers actually capture them? In 1215, rebel barons seized Rochester Castle to resist King John. John had to get it back. What followed is one of the most unusual stories in military history.

⚔️ Siege Weapons — The Attacker's Arsenal
  • 🪨 Trebuchet — a counterweight catapult that could hurl 150kg boulders over 300 metres
  • 🪨 Mangonel — a smaller, faster catapult for sustained fire at walls and towers
  • 🔨 Battering ram — a heavy tree trunk swung on chains to smash through gates
  • 🪜 Siege tower — a wooden tower on wheels, rolled up to the walls
  • ⛏️ Mining — tunnels dug beneath corner towers to collapse the foundations
  • ⏳ Siege — simply surround the castle and wait for the defenders to starve
⚔️ The 40 Fat Pigs — Step by Step
Step 1: Siege engines hurl massive boulders at Rochester's walls day and night. The walls hold — they are 3 metres thick at their base.
Step 2: John orders his miners to dig tunnels beneath the south-east tower, weakening its foundations from below.
Step 3: John issues one of history's strangest military orders: "Send to us with all speed, by day and night, forty of the fattest pigs of the sort least good for eating, that we may take them for bringing fire beneath the tower."
Step 4: The pig fat is packed into the tunnel and set alight. It burns at such intense heat — far hotter than wood alone — that the wooden props collapse and the entire corner tower comes crashing down.
Result: Rochester Castle falls. 40 fat pigs achieved what months of conventional siege warfare could not.
📜 King John's written order, 1215 (from the historical record)
"Send to us with all speed, by day and night, forty of the fattest pigs of the sort least good for eating, that we may take them for bringing fire beneath the tower."

One of history's most unusual royal orders. Apply WACA: Is this a primary source? What does it reveal about how King John approached military engineering problems?

🎯 Activity 4: The Siege of Rochester — Tactical Analysis
  • You are King John's military advisor in 1215. Rank the six siege methods above (trebuchet, mangonel, battering ram, siege tower, mining, starvation) from most to least effective against Rochester Castle — and justify each ranking.
  • Why did John choose to use mining AND the pigs together, rather than just one method? What does this reveal about medieval military intelligence?
  • Was the 40-pigs solution: (a) obvious — anyone could have thought of it; (b) brilliant — only a creative thinker would try it; (c) lucky — it might easily have failed? Use evidence to justify your answer.
  • 💎 Platinum: 'The Siege of Rochester shows that medieval warfare was more sophisticated than popular history suggests.' How far do you agree? Use specific evidence from the siege.

📉 Why Did Castles Decline?

For almost 300 years, stone castles were virtually impregnable. But by the 15th century, two developments had made them obsolete almost overnight — and the change was permanent.

💥
Gunpowder Changes Everything
Cannons firing iron cannonballs could batter down stone walls that had previously withstood decades of siege. A wall that could resist a trebuchet for months might fall to a cannon in days. The physics of defence changed permanently.
🕊️
England Becomes More Peaceful
As royal authority strengthened and internal wars decreased, the need for impregnable private fortresses declined. The rich began building comfortable manor houses with large windows instead — comfort over defence.

🎯 Research Project — Bronze to Platinum

🏰 Were Castles More About Fear Than Fighting?
You are advising William the Conqueror in 1070. Write a report arguing whether castles should be primarily military fortifications, symbols of power — or both. Use specific evidence.
🥈 SilverCompare motte-and-bailey and stone keep castles across four categories: speed of construction, defensive strength, cost, and symbolic impact. Which overall was more effective?
🥇 Gold'Castles were more important as symbols of Norman power than as military fortifications.' Use evidence from the Rochester siege AND the Tower of London to argue both sides. Reach a justified conclusion.
💎 PlatinumApply full WACA to the Tower of London as a historical source. Then write a 16-mark essay: 'How far did the building of castles explain why William successfully kept control of England after 1066?' Use PEEL structure throughout.

📋 Project Overview — What You Need to Do

Over the next 3–4 weeks (including lessons and homework), you will create a structured research project on castles in medieval England (1066–1600). This is your opportunity to investigate, research, and present your findings at the highest level you can achieve.

📚 Key Vocabulary — Use These Accurately in Your Project
Motte & Bailey: Early castle: wooden tower on raised mound beside an enclosed yard (bailey)
Square Keep: A tall, strong stone tower at the heart of the castle
Moat: A water-filled ditch dug around a castle for defence
Battlements: The notched wall along the top where defenders stood
Gatehouse: The strongly guarded entrance to a castle
Portcullis: A heavy iron grille dropped to block the gateway
Siege: Surrounding and attacking a castle to force surrender
Trebuchet: A counterweight catapult hurling heavy boulders at walls
Feudal System: The medieval order of king, barons, knights and peasants
Concentric Castle: Rings of walls within walls — brought back from the Crusades
🎯 Activity 1: COMPULSORY — Where to Build a Castle?

Group Discussion, then Individual Report

You are a group of Norman lords loyal to King William. You must choose the BEST of five sites (A–E) to build your castle at Maintown — northern England's key trading centre of about 1,000 people.

  • Site A — Coast: Easy to supply by ship — but open to seaborne raids.
  • Site B — Marshland: Wet ground provides defence — but soil is unstable and disease spreads quickly.
  • Site C — Hill by the river: High ground gives early warning and the river forms a natural moat. Steep climb for attackers. (The 'correct' answer — but you must justify it!)
  • Site D — Flat open land: Easy to build on — but no natural defences.
  • Site E — Forest edge: Plenty of timber and cover — but trees could hide an approaching enemy.

For EACH site: list at least 2 advantages AND 2 disadvantages. Then write a 150-word report for William the Conqueror recommending the BEST site. Include: defensive advantages, water supply, visibility, access to supplies, any risks.

What makes a good castle site? Consider: High ground (early warning) · River (water + natural moat) · Main roads (control trade and movement) · Nearby towns (tax revenue and labour)

🎯 Activity 2: Motte-and-Bailey Castles (Essential Section)

Research and present a section on motte-and-bailey castles. Your section MUST include:

  • A labelled diagram showing: motte, bailey, keep, ditch, drawbridge, palisade, and gatehouse
  • Why William chose motte-and-bailey immediately after 1066 (speed was critical — as few as 8 days to build)
  • The key materials used and their weaknesses (wood → fire, rot)
  • Why stone later replaced wood
  • At least ONE specific example — e.g. Windsor Castle began as a motte-and-bailey
  • Key fact: By 1086, over 100 motte-and-bailey castles had been built across England
🎯 Activity 3: Choose Your Own Sections

Choose at least 3 additional sections from the list below. Each section should answer a specific question about castles:

  • 🏰 Stone Keep Castles: How did stone keeps improve on motte-and-bailey? Include the Tower of London as a case study. Explain: spiral stairs, murder holes, portcullis, battlements.
  • ⚔️ Siege Weapons and Tactics: Research trebuchet, mangonel, battering ram, mining. Include the Siege of Rochester (1215) — what happened and why was it significant?
  • 🏡 Life Inside a Castle: Who lived there? What did they eat? How was it heated? What entertainment was available? Who was the 'gong farmer'?
  • 🔄 Concentric Castles: What are they? Why were round towers better than square ones? Research Beaumaris Castle.
  • 📉 Why Did Castles Decline? What role did gunpowder and cannons play? How did increased peace affect castle design?
  • 🏰 A Specific Castle: Choose Tower of London, Windsor, Edinburgh, or one near you. Research: why built there, how it changed, who owned it, any sieges.
🎯 Activity 4: Research Tips and Rules
  • ❌ DO NOT copy and paste text from any source — that is plagiarism. Always put everything into your own words.
  • ✅ You CAN copy images, maps, and diagrams — and you should! Label them carefully.
  • ✅ Use clear headings for each section. Organise your project into chapters.
  • ✅ Support every claim with evidence — 'according to [source]' or 'for example...'
  • ✅ Include: animations, videos, interviews for Gold and Platinum level
  • 📊 Project Levels: Bronze = complete Location activity + worksheets · Silver = 5+ sections with images · Gold = depth, timelines, source analysis · Platinum = outstanding research on a real castle with WACA analysis of at least 2 sources

“Castles were not merely defensive structures — they were statements of power, permanence and dominance carved into the English landscape.”— Marc Morris, The Norman Conquest, 2012
⚡ Quick Quiz
What was the main purpose of the motte-and-bailey castle built by the Normans after 1066?
🧠

📅 Year 7 · Medieval England · Topic 2 · 16 Lessons

🎭 Medieval
Entertainment & Life

Leisure was not a right in medieval England — it was a rare gift. The Church structured life around feast days (from which the word 'holiday' derives, via 'holy day'), and only during these precious windows did ordinary people have time to play. What they chose to do reveals as much about the social order as any written chronicle.

📅 Key Date1363 — Edward III bans mob football
🔑 Key ConceptChange & continuity
📖 Key TermSumptuary laws
🎯 Key SkillSource analysis · WACA
🔊 Listen:

🎮 Could You Have Fun in the Middle Ages?

Ordinary people did not have 'holidays'. Instead, there were feast days through the year — Easter, May Day, Midsummer's Eve, Christmas, and various Saints' days. After church, they had some free time. Our word 'holiday' comes from the term 'holy day'. Here are the most popular pastimes:

🎳
Bowling / Skittles
Players took turns to knock down skittles with balls.
✅ Still played
⚽
Mob Football
No rules, no referee — up to 500 players, goals miles apart, pig's bladder ball.
✅ Evolved → football
🌰
Conkers
Became popular after 1066. Drill a cobnut, thread a string — smash opponent's nut.
✅ Still played
⛸️
Ice Skating
Sharpened animal bones strapped to boots. A boy's skeleton was found with skates still on.
✅ Still played
🎾
Real Tennis (Nobles)
Wooden ball over a rope. Balls struck hard enough to kill on impact.
✅ Still exists (rare)
🐓
Cock Fighting
Birds forced to fight with metal spurs. People bet on result.
❌ Now illegal
🐻
Bear Baiting
A bear chained while dogs attacked. People bet on outcome.
❌ Now illegal
🏹
Archery
Boys practised from a very young age — armies always needed archers.
✅ Olympic sport

⚽ Football Through the Ages

According to one account, the game began when an English mob kicked around the head of a defeated Danish invader. By the 12th century, mob football was firmly established — and deeply controversial. It was banned more than 30 times between 1314 and 1667.

📋 The (Non-) Rules
  • 👥 Up to 500 players at once
  • 🐷 Ball = pig's bladder stuffed with dried peas
  • 📏 Goals might be several miles apart
  • ⚖️ Any method permitted "except murder"
  • 📅 Usually played on Shrove Tuesday
🚫 The Bans
1314Lord Mayor of London bans it — ignored!
1321Pope pardons player who killed his opponent
1331Edward III — first royal decree banning it
166730+ bans total — still played everywhere
📜 Source A — William Fitzstephen, c.1174 (first written description)
After lunch all the youth of the city go out into the fields to take part in the ball game. The students of each school have their own ball. The workers from each city craft also carry balls. Older citizens, fathers and wealthy men come on horseback to watch.
c.1174 — the first written description of mob football in London on Shrove Tuesday.
📜 Source B — A monk's description
a devilish pastime. More a bloody murdering practice than a sport.

🎵 Music, Minstrels & Miracle Plays

The Middle Ages had its own pop songs and dance music. Wandering minstrels were the medieval equivalent of social media — bringing news and entertainment from town to town. Miracle plays drew crowds across 127 different towns.

🎶 Sumer Is Icumen In (13th century)
Summer has come in, Loudly sing, Cuckoo!
The seed grows and the meadow blooms,
And the wood springs anew. Sing, Cuckoo!
The ewe bleats after the lamb,
Merrily sing, Cuckoo!

One of the oldest surviving English songs — passed down without written notation.

👗 Fashion, Status & Sumptuary Laws

👑 Wealthy Nobles
  • ✨ Velvet, silk, fur, fine linen
  • 🎨 Vivid dyes (red=love, yellow=anger, grey=sadness)
  • 👒 Steeple hats — nearly a metre tall in the 1480s
  • 👠 Poulaines — toes so pointed, tied back to ankle
🌾 Ordinary Peasants
  • 🧶 Coarse wool or linen tunics
  • 🟫 Muted colours: grey, brown, dark green
  • 👞 Some owned no shoes at all
  • 🏠 Clothing was handmade and repaired endlessly

Sumptuary Laws were royal decrees forbidding lower classes from wearing fabrics, colours or styles associated with nobility. Their existence is revealing: ordinary people were trying to imitate their social betters — and the ruling class found this threatening enough to require Parliament to stop it.

📖 The English Language After 1066

After the Conquest, English became the third most important language in England — below French and Latin. King Harold was the last monarch whose first language was English. By the 14th century, Geoffrey Chaucer was writing The Canterbury Tales in something very close to modern English.

⚔️ Anglo-Saxon
drink · father · ground · love · name · son · wife
🛡️ Norse (Viking)
anger · gate · neck · ugly · you · oaf · skin
🇫🇷 Norman French
archer · castle · city · judge · prison · sausage · sugar

🍽️ Come Dine With Me!

Breakfast
4–6am
🍞 Bread · 🍺 Ale
Dinner
10am
🍞 Bread · 🧀 Cheese · 🧅 Onion · 🥚 Eggs · 🍺 Ale
Supper
4pm
🍞 Bread · 🐟 Fish · 🥣 Broth

Peasants ate hedgehog, squirrel, pike and eel without hesitation. No potatoes — they only reached England in the 1500s. Everyone drank weak ale — water was generally unsafe. A peasant's diet was probably healthier than many modern diets: no refined sugar, no processed food.

🎯 Activities

🎭 Activity 1: Medieval Entertainment Programme

You are the royal entertainment organiser for a Norman castle feast in 1250. Plan a full-day programme from dawn to midnight.

  • At least SIX different activities
  • For each: who could participate (nobles/peasants/everyone) and WHY social class affected access
  • Mark any activities banned by the Church and explain why
  • Compare: which still exist today?
⚽ Activity 2: Football Through the Ages
  • Use specific evidence from what you have read above to support all your answers. Apply the WACA framework to any sources and use precise historical vocabulary.
  • Apply the WACA framework to any sources you use.
  • Share your response with the class and compare your conclusions.
📖 Activity 3: The Story of English
  • Why did English become the THIRD most important language in England after 1066?
  • Find FIVE modern English words that came from Norman French
  • Why do we have TWO words for the same thing (pig/pork, cow/beef)? What does this tell us about Norman society?
🎥 Dan Snow · The History Guy · Crash Course
🎥 Dan Snow: Life in Medieval England🎥 The History Guy: Medieval Entertainment🎥 Crash Course: Medieval Life
📊 Medieval Entertainment Master PPT📁 Medieval Life Pack (PDF)📊 Football Through the Ages📄 Student Worksheet Pack🔗 Britannica: The Middle Ages
🏛️ Historian's Verdict“Medieval people had a sophisticated culture — but access to that culture depended entirely on what class you were born into.”— Historian's perspective
💡 Did You Know?
The word 'holiday' comes from 'holy day' — the ONLY rest medieval peasants got all year.
⚡ Quick Quiz
What is the origin of the modern English word 'holiday'?
🧠

⚓ Year 8 — Slavery, Industry & Empire

The transatlantic slave trade, Industrial Revolution, Victorian living conditions, and the British Empire.

📅⭐🌍🤖
📘 📝 Extended Writing📘 💬 Debate📘 🌐 Global Perspectives📘 🔍 Source Evaluation🔬 Analysis⚖️ Evaluation🔗 Making Connections💪 Resilience👥 CollaborationSDG 10: Reduced InequalitiesSDG 16: Peace & Justice⚖️ AI Ethics

⛓️ What Was the Transatlantic Slave Trade?

Between 1500 and 1900, approximately 12.5 million Africans were forcibly transported across the Atlantic Ocean to work as enslaved people on plantations in the Americas and Caribbean. It was one of the largest forced migrations in human history — and one of the most profitable enterprises ever organised. Britain became the dominant slave-trading nation by the 18th century, with ships sailing from Bristol, Liverpool and London.

📊 The Triangular Trade
LEG 1 — Britain to Africa: Ships carried goods (guns, cloth, alcohol) to trade for enslaved people.
LEG 2 — The Middle Passage: Enslaved Africans were transported across the Atlantic in horrific conditions. The journey took 6–8 weeks. Up to 1 in 5 died during the crossing.
LEG 3 — Americas to Britain: Ships returned laden with sugar, cotton, tobacco and rum — all produced by enslaved labour.
💰 Who Profited?
  • 🚢 Ship owners and merchants in Bristol, Liverpool, London
  • 🌾 Plantation owners in the Caribbean and Americas
  • 🏦 Banks and insurance companies that funded the trade
  • 🏭 British manufacturers whose goods were traded for people
  • ☕ British consumers who enjoyed cheap sugar, coffee, cotton

🚢 The Middle Passage — A Journey of Suffering

The Middle Passage was the name given to the second leg of the triangular trade — the crossing of the Atlantic. Enslaved people were packed into ships so tightly they could not stand, turn over, or even breathe properly. The Brookes diagram (1787) — showing how 454 people were crammed into a ship designed for 450 — became one of the most powerful pieces of abolitionist propaganda in history.

📜 The Brookes Diagram, 1787 — Visual Source
The diagram showed enslaved people packed into a ship with no room to move, lying on shelves barely 18 inches apart. It was produced by abolitionists to show the British public what the slave trade actually looked like.

Apply WACA: Who produced this? For what AUDIENCE? In what CONTEXT (1787 — abolition movement growing)? With what AIM? Does knowing the abolitionists made it make it MORE or LESS useful as evidence?

📜 Olaudah Equiano, 'The Interesting Narrative', 1789
"The closeness of the place, and the heat of the climate, added to the number in the ship, which was so crowded that each had scarcely room to turn himself, almost suffocated us. The shrieks of the women, and the groans of the dying, rendered the whole a scene of horror almost inconceivable."

Olaudah Equiano was enslaved as a child and later bought his freedom. He became a leading abolitionist. Apply WACA: How does knowing he was enslaved affect the usefulness and reliability of this account?

🎯 Activity 1: The Triangular Trade
  • Use specific evidence from what you have read above to support all your answers. Apply the WACA framework to any sources and use precise historical vocabulary.
  • Apply the WACA framework to any sources you use.
  • Share your response with the class and compare your conclusions.
  • Draw the Triangular Trade as a diagram showing all three legs. For each leg, write: what was transported, who benefited, who suffered.
  • Apply WACA to the Brookes Diagram (1787) — who produced it? For whom? In what context? With what aim? Does knowing abolitionists made it make it MORE or LESS useful as evidence?
  • Write a paragraph: "Who was most responsible for the continuation of the transatlantic slave trade — merchants, plantation owners, or governments?" Justify your answer.

🌍 Life Under Slavery

Once enslaved people arrived in the Americas and Caribbean, they were sold at auction — separated from their families and communities forever. On sugar, tobacco, and cotton plantations, they worked from sunrise to sunset under brutal conditions. They had no legal rights, could not marry, and their children were also enslaved from birth.

⏰
Working Conditions
Enslaved people worked 16-18 hour days during harvest. Sugar cane cutting was particularly dangerous — machetes, crushing mills, and boiling sugar caused frequent severe injuries.
⚖️
No Legal Rights
Enslaved people were legally classified as property, not people. They could be sold, beaten, or killed by their owners with minimal legal consequence.
👨‍👩‍👧
Family Separation
Families were routinely separated at auction. Children were sold away from parents. Husbands and wives were split and sent to different plantations miles apart.
✊
Resistance
Despite brutal conditions, enslaved people resisted constantly: working slowly, breaking tools, running away, and organising rebellions. Haiti's revolution (1791) overthrew French colonial rule entirely.
📜 Mary Prince, 'The History of Mary Prince', 1831
"I was flogged to make me work faster. It is not possible to describe the feelings of this horrible punishment. I know what it is to see a person flogged until the blood runs down. The owner of the plantation was very cruel."

Mary Prince was the first enslaved Black woman to publish her autobiography in Britain. Apply WACA: Why is her account particularly significant? What limitations might it have?

🎯 Activity 2: Life Under Slavery — Source Analysis
  • Apply WACA to BOTH Equiano's account AND Mary Prince's account above. For each: Who? Audience? Context? Aim?
  • Compare the two accounts. What do they tell us about the experience of slavery that the Brookes Diagram CANNOT tell us?
  • Write your WACA judgement for each source: 'This source is useful for understanding [X] because... however it is limited because...'
  • 💎 Platinum: 'Eyewitness accounts of enslaved people are the most important sources we have for understanding the slave trade.' How far do you agree? Consider both their strengths and their limitations.

⚖️ The Abolition Movement — Fighting Back

The campaign to abolish the slave trade was one of the first great mass protest movements in British history. It used petitions, pamphlets, boycotts of slave-grown sugar, and Parliamentary lobbying over decades. William Wilberforce led the Parliamentary campaign, while Olaudah Equiano and other formerly enslaved people gave the movement a powerful human voice. Women played a crucial role through the Ladies' Anti-Slavery Society and sugar boycotts.

✅ Key Abolition Victories
  • 📅 1772: Somerset Case — enslaved people could not be removed from England against their will
  • 📅 1807: Slave Trade Act — abolished the buying and selling of enslaved people in British ships
  • 📅 1833: Slavery Abolition Act — freed enslaved people across the British Empire
  • 📅 1838: Full emancipation — the "apprenticeship" period ended
❌ The Painful Aftermath
  • 💰 Plantation owners were paid £20 million compensation (£17 billion today) — the enslaved people received nothing
  • ⛓️ Freed people were forced to work as "apprentices" for their former enslavers for 6 more years
  • 🌍 The legacy of slavery — racism, poverty, inequality — continues to affect communities today
🎯 Activity 3: The Abolition Campaign
  • Create a timeline of the key events in the abolition campaign from 1770 to 1838. For each event, explain WHO was responsible and WHY it was significant.
  • Who deserves the most credit for abolition: William Wilberforce, Olaudah Equiano, the women's boycott movement, or the enslaved people who rebelled? Rank them and justify your ranking.
  • Apply WACA to the Brookes diagram: Who made it? For whom? When? With what aim? Does knowing abolitionists made it change how you use it as evidence?
  • 🥇 Gold: 'The Slavery Abolition Act of 1833 was not a true act of justice.' How far do you agree? Consider: the compensation to enslavers, the apprenticeship system, and what happened afterwards.

🌐 Slavery Today — Modern Slavery

The formal abolition of slavery did not end all forms of forced labour and exploitation. Modern slavery — including human trafficking, forced labour, and debt bondage — affects an estimated 40 million people worldwide today. The Modern Slavery Act (2015) made the UK the first country in the world to require companies to report on steps taken to eliminate slavery from their supply chains.

🎯 Activity 4: Connections Across Time
  • Research: what is modern slavery? How does it differ from the transatlantic slave trade? How is it similar?
  • The UN Sustainable Development Goals include SDG 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth and SDG 10: Reduced Inequalities. How does the legacy of the slave trade connect to these goals today?
  • 💎 Platinum: 'The legacy of the transatlantic slave trade is still felt in British society and around the world today.' Compile evidence for and against this argument. Write a 400-word response.
🎯 Activity 5: Bronze to Platinum Research Project
  • 🥉 Bronze: List 5 key facts about the transatlantic slave trade, including dates, numbers, and key figures involved in both the trade AND the abolition movement.
  • 🥈 Silver: Write a 200-word account of the Middle Passage from the perspective of an enslaved person. Use historical evidence — do NOT invent details that contradict the facts. Apply WACA to your own account: who are you? What is your aim? How might this affect your reliability?
  • 🥇 Gold: Explain the role of THREE individuals in the abolition movement: Olaudah Equiano, Granville Sharp, William Wilberforce. Who was most important — and why? Use specific evidence for each.
  • 💎 Platinum: 'British economic interests were more important than moral arguments in abolishing the slave trade in 1807.' How far do you agree? Write a 400-word essay using specific evidence. Consider: Was abolition economically rational by 1807? Was the moral case strong enough alone?
  • Bronze: List 5 key facts about the transatlantic slave trade, including dates, numbers, and key figures.
  • Silver: Write a 200-word account of the Middle Passage from the perspective of an enslaved person. Use historical evidence — do NOT invent details that contradict the facts.
  • Gold: Explain the role of THREE individuals in the abolition movement: Olaudah Equiano, Granville Sharp, William Wilberforce. Who was most important?
  • Platinum: "British economic interests were more important than moral arguments in abolishing the slave trade in 1807." How far do you agree? Write a 400-word essay using specific evidence.
⚡ Quick Quiz
What was the name of the famous diagram that showed enslaved people packed into a slave ship, used by abolitionists?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “No one can read about the slave trade without moral revulsion. But it was a deliberate, calculated economic system — not an accident.” — James Walvin, 2007
💡 Did You Know?
Each enslaved person on the Brookes was allocated just 16 inches width.
🧠
