💡 Did You Know?
Five-Year Plans increased Soviet steel from 4 to 18 million tonnes 1928-1940.
📅⭐🌍🤖
📘 🔍 Source Evaluation📘 📝 Extended Writing📘 💬 Debate📘 🌐 Global Perspectives🔬 Analysis⚖️ Evaluation🔗 Making Connections💪 Resilience🎯 Self-awarenessSDG 16: Peace & Justice🔍 AI Critical Thinking⚖️ AI Ethics🛠️ AI Tools

🌏 Why Did the USA Get Involved in Vietnam?

After the French defeat at Dien Bien Phu (1954), Vietnam was temporarily divided at the 17th parallel — communist North under Ho Chi Minh, pro-Western South under Diem. The USA feared that if South Vietnam fell to communism, the rest of South-East Asia would follow — the Domino Theory. What began as financial aid and military advisors would escalate into America's longest and most divisive war.

🀱
Domino Theory
Eisenhower (1954): if one country falls to communism, its neighbours will follow like dominoes. Vietnam was seen as the crucial first domino in South-East Asia.
💰
Financial Aid
USA began funding the French war effort from 1950. By 1954, paying 80% of French war costs — yet France still lost at Dien Bien Phu.
🪖
Military Advisors
Kennedy sent 16,000 'military advisors' by 1963. Officially not combat troops — actually training the ARVN (Army of the Republic of Vietnam).
💥
Gulf of Tonkin (1964)
Two reported attacks on US destroyers (one probably never happened). Congress passed the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution — giving Johnson blank-cheque authority to escalate without declaring war.
📜 President Eisenhower — press conference, April 1954
"You have a row of dominoes set up, you knock over the first one, and what will happen to the last one is a certainty — it will go over very quickly."

Apply WACA: this is the Domino Theory explained by the president himself. Was it an accurate analysis — or a political justification for intervention?

🎯 Activity 1: Why Did the USA Escalate Involvement?
  • Create a timeline of US escalation: 1950 (financial aid) → 1955 (military advisors) → 1964 (Gulf of Tonkin) → 1965 (first combat troops) → 1968 (peak 540,000 troops). For each step, explain WHY the USA went further.
  • Was the Gulf of Tonkin incident a genuine attack or a pretext? What evidence exists on both sides?
  • 🥇 'The Domino Theory was the main reason the USA became involved in Vietnam.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 6-mark answer using specific evidence.

⚔️ US Military Tactics — Search and Destroy

By 1965, 540,000 US troops were in Vietnam. General Westmoreland's strategy was "attrition" — killing enough North Vietnamese and Viet Cong to break their will to fight. It failed — because the enemy's will to fight proved inexhaustible.

🔥 US Tactics
  • 🚁 Search & Destroy: helicopter-borne raids on suspected Viet Cong villages
  • 🌿 Agent Orange: herbicide defoliation — 80 million litres dropped, destroying jungle cover and crops
  • 💣 Napalm bombing: incendiary gel — stuck to skin and burned at 1,000°C
  • 🏗️ Strategic Hamlets: moving peasants into fortified villages — failed, created resentment
  • 📊 Body Count: military success measured by enemy dead — encouraged false reporting
🌿 Viet Cong Tactics
  • 🕳️ Cu Chi Tunnels: 250km network — storage, hospitals, troop movement
  • 💣 Booby traps: punji sticks, tripwires — caused 11% of US casualties
  • 🌳 Guerrilla warfare: attack and melt into civilian population
  • 🏘️ Hearts & Minds: political work among peasants to win local support
  • 🛣️ Ho Chi Minh Trail: 1,000km supply route through Laos/Cambodia
📜 Philip Caputo, US Marine, A Rumour of War, 1977
"We were not in Vietnam to win. We were there to demonstrate that America was willing to fight, even if it couldn't win. And we were killing people — lots of people — but nothing was changing."

Apply WACA: Caputo was a participant — eyewitness value high, but written 12 years after events. How does PTSD and retrospective analysis affect reliability?

🎯 Activity 2: Why Did US Tactics Fail?
  • For each US tactic (Search & Destroy, Agent Orange, Strategic Hamlets, napalm), explain: what it was designed to achieve AND why it failed.
  • How did the Viet Cong's guerrilla tactics neutralise America's technological advantages?
  • 🥇 'US military tactics in Vietnam were the main reason the USA failed to win the war.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer with a balanced evaluation.

📺 The Tet Offensive and the Credibility Gap (1968)

On 31 January 1968 — the Vietnamese New Year (Tet) — 85,000 Viet Cong and North Vietnamese troops simultaneously attacked 100+ cities across South Vietnam. US and South Vietnamese forces regained all the ground within weeks. But the political damage was permanent.

📜 Walter Cronkite — CBS Evening News broadcast, February 1968
"It seems now more certain than ever that the bloody experience of Vietnam is to end in a stalemate... To say that we are closer to victory today is to believe, in the face of the evidence, the optimists who have been wrong in the past."

Cronkite was 'the most trusted man in America.' When Johnson heard this broadcast, he reportedly said: 'If I've lost Cronkite, I've lost Middle America.' Apply WACA to this source.

🎯 Activity 3: How Did Tet Change Everything?
  • Before Tet, Johnson had been saying the USA was 'winning' — the 'light at the end of the tunnel.' How did Tet expose this as the Credibility Gap?
  • Why was Tet a military failure for the Viet Cong but a political victory? What does this tell us about the nature of the Vietnam War?
  • 🥇 'The Tet Offensive (1968) was the turning point of the Vietnam War.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer.

✈️ Withdrawal and the Legacy of Vietnam

Nixon's strategy of "Vietnamisation" — gradually handing the fighting back to South Vietnamese forces while withdrawing US troops — preserved American pride but could not prevent the inevitable. Saigon fell to communist forces on 30 April 1975.

🔄
Vietnamisation
Nixon withdraws US troops gradually — 540,000 in 1968 to 50,000 by 1972. But the ARVN cannot hold without US support.
💣
Cambodia Bombing
Nixon secretly bombs Cambodia to destroy Ho Chi Minh Trail. Leaked to press — triggers massive antiwar protests including Kent State (4 students killed by National Guard).
📋
Paris Peace Accords (1973)
Ceasefire agreement — US troops withdraw. South Vietnam government told to survive alone. It cannot.
🏃
Fall of Saigon (1975)
North Vietnamese forces enter Saigon. US helicopters evacuate the last Americans from the embassy rooftop. The defining image of American defeat.
🎯 Activity 4: Why Did the USA Fail in Vietnam?
  • Use specific evidence from what you have read above to support all your answers. Apply the WACA framework to any sources and use precise historical vocabulary.
  • Apply the WACA framework to any sources you use.
  • Share your response with the class and compare your conclusions.
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “We did not have the best equipment. But we had something the Americans could not buy — we were fighting for our home.” — North Vietnamese veteran, 1990
🧠

📅⭐🌍🤖
📘 📊 Data Analysis📘 🔍 Source Evaluation📘 📝 Extended Writing🔬 Analysis⚖️ Evaluation🔗 Making Connections💪 Resilience🎯 Self-awarenessSDG 3: Good HealthSDG 4: Quality Education🔍 AI Critical Thinking⚖️ AI Ethics🛠️ AI Tools

🏥 Medicine in the Mid-Nineteenth Century (c.1848)

In 1848, medicine was a dangerous profession — not because doctors were incompetent, but because they lacked the fundamental knowledge that would transform their field. Without germ theory, surgeons operated in dirty aprons. Without anaesthetics, speed was the surgeon's only mercy. Without antiseptics, patients who survived the knife often died of infection. The next century would change everything.

❌ What Doctors Didn't Know (1848)
  • 🦠 Cause of disease: still believed in miasma (bad air)
  • 😷 Infection: surgeons operated in street clothes with bare hands
  • 💊 Antibiotics: had not been invented
  • 🩸 Blood types: not discovered until 1901
  • 📊 Statistics: no systematic data collection on outcomes
✅ What WAS Improving (1848)
  • 💤 Anaesthetics: Simpson uses chloroform 1847 — pain-free operations begin
  • 💉 Vaccination: Jenner's smallpox vaccine (1796) now widely used
  • 🏥 Nightingale: nursing reforms in Scutari during Crimean War (1854–56)
  • 🚰 Chadwick: Sanitary Report 1842 — first evidence for public health reform
  • 📊 Snow: broad Street Pump 1854 — proves cholera is waterborne
🎯 Activity 1: How Much Had Medicine Changed by 1848?
  • Use specific evidence from what you have read above to support all your answers. Apply the WACA framework to any sources and use precise historical vocabulary.
  • Apply the WACA framework to any sources you use.
  • Share your response with the class and compare your conclusions.

🔬 Pasteur, Koch and the Germ Theory Revolution (1860–1905)

The single most important development in the history of medicine was the Germ Theory — the discovery that specific microorganisms cause specific diseases. Once this was established, everything else followed: specific vaccines, specific treatments, targeted surgery.

1861Pasteur's Germ Theory — published his findings that microorganisms cause fermentation and decay. Disproves spontaneous generation.
1867Lister introduces carbolic acid antiseptic — surgical death rates from infection drop from 46% to 15%.
1875Koch identifies specific bacteria causing anthrax and tuberculosis — proving specific germs cause specific diseases.
1882Koch's postulates — systematic method for proving a microorganism causes a disease.
1880s–90sEhrlich and the 'magic bullet' — searching for chemical compounds that kill specific bacteria without harming the patient.
1909Salvarsan 606 — first chemotherapy drug, effective against syphilis. Proof that magic bullets were possible.
📜 Joseph Lister on his use of antiseptic technique, 1867
"All that is requisite is to prevent the access of the minute particles in the atmosphere to the wound... by covering with a material capable of killing those organisms."

Apply WACA: Lister was defending a controversial new technique — many doctors resisted it. What does resistance to Lister tell us about how medical knowledge changes?

🎯 Activity 2: Pasteur, Koch, Lister — Who Made the Biggest Contribution?
  • For each figure — Pasteur, Koch, Lister, Ehrlich — explain: their specific contribution AND its significance for medicine.
  • Why did it take so long for Pasteur's Germ Theory to be accepted? What does this tell us about how scientific knowledge changes?
  • 🥇 'Koch's work in identifying specific bacteria was more important than Pasteur's Germ Theory.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 6-mark answer with specific evidence.

🏛️ Government Action and World Wars (1905–1948)

Medical knowledge alone cannot improve public health — governments must act on it. The early 20th century saw the British government take unprecedented responsibility for citizens' health, driven partly by reformers, partly by the shocking state of army recruits during the Boer War.

🏫
Liberal Reforms (1906–11)
Free school meals, medical inspections, old age pensions, National Insurance. First time British government accepted responsibility for citizens' health.
🩺
World War One (1914–18)
Blood transfusions developed, plastic surgery pioneered, X-rays used on the front line, understanding of shell shock begins.
💊
Penicillin (1928–45)
Fleming discovers penicillin by accident (1928). Florey and Chain develop it into a usable drug by 1940. Mass-produced for D-Day — saves hundreds of thousands of lives.
🏥
NHS (1948)
Attlee's Labour government creates the National Health Service — free healthcare for all at the point of need. Life expectancy rises from 47 (1900) to 68 (1950).
📜 Aneurin Bevan, Minister of Health, on the creation of the NHS, 1948
"The NHS will last as long as there are folk left with the faith to fight for it."

Apply WACA: Bevan was the politician who created the NHS. Is this a primary or secondary source? How does his role affect its reliability as evidence of why the NHS was needed?

🎯 Activity 3: How Did the Government Change Medicine?
  • Why did the Boer War shock the British government into introducing the Liberal Reforms? What did it reveal about the nation's health?
  • How did each World War contribute to medical progress? Consider both the injuries treated AND the technologies developed.
  • 🥇 'The creation of the National Health Service (1948) was the most important factor in improving British public health in the 20th century.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 16-mark essay considering: the NHS, penicillin, public health legislation, and individual medical discoveries.

🧬 Modern Medicine (c.1920–c.1948) — The Age of Breakthroughs

The mid-20th century saw medicine transformed by a combination of factors: government funding, technological innovation, the demands of war, and occasional brilliant luck. The story of penicillin perfectly illustrates how chance, individual genius, government support, and technology must ALL combine for a medical revolution.

📜 Fleming's discovery of penicillin — reconstructed account, 1928
"When I woke up just after dawn on 28 September 1928, I certainly didn't plan to revolutionise all medicine by discovering the world's first antibiotic... But I suppose that was exactly what I did."

Apply WACA: this quote is widely cited but possibly apocryphal. How does it matter whether Fleming said this — or whether it was constructed later?

🧫 The Penicillin Story
  • 🔬 1928: Fleming notices mould killing bacteria — publishes finding, but cannot stabilise the drug
  • ⏸️ 1928–39: Penicillin ignored for a decade — no technology or funding to develop it
  • 🧪 1940: Florey and Chain (Oxford) develop a usable form — test on a police constable (he dies when supplies run out)
  • 🇺🇸 1943: USA mass-produces penicillin — government funding and wartime urgency make it possible
  • ⚔️ 1944: Used in D-Day invasion — revolutionises wound treatment
🎯 Activity 4: Factors in Medical Progress — What Matters Most?
  • Use specific evidence from what you have read above to support all your answers. Apply the WACA framework to any sources and use precise historical vocabulary.
  • Apply the WACA framework to any sources you use.
  • Share your response with the class and compare your conclusions.
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “The NHS was not a gift from politicians. It was the realisation of a right — that illness should not mean poverty.” — Aneurin Bevan, 1948
🧠

Click any card to open a full revision sub-page with key dates, terms, exam tips and practice questions.

⚡ iGCSE Exam Practice Questions

Cycle through 6, 8 and 16-mark questions. Write or speak your answer, then click Get Feedback for personalised AI guidance.

📘 Paper 1 — Cold War & Stalin
📝 Mark scheme tip
📗 Paper 2 — Vietnam & Medicine
📝 Mark scheme tip

What is History?

📅 Key Dates
Key methodWACA: Who, Audience, Context, Aim
Key skillChronology: BC counts BACKWARDS
Key conceptPRIMARY = created at the time. SECONDARY = created later
📖 Key Terms
Primary SourceSecondary SourceWACAChronologyCausationConsequenceChangeContinuity
💡 Exam Tips
💡 Apply WACA to EVERY source before judging its usefulness.
💡 BC counts BACKWARDS — 100 BC is BEFORE 50 BC.
💡 IMMEDIATE cause = the spark. UNDERLYING causes = deeper long-term reasons.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe WACA as a method for analysing historical sources. Include: what each letter stands for, one example for each letter, and why applying WACA matters for historians.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why historians need BOTH primary AND secondary sources when studying the past. Use two specific examples from the sources you have studied this year.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'A primary source is always more reliable than a secondary source.' How far do you agree? Consider: what makes a source reliable, how WACA reveals limitations, and give specific examples.

Norman Conquest 1066

📅 Key Dates
14 Oct 1066Battle of Hastings — Harold killed, William wins
25 Sep 1066Battle of Stamford Bridge — Harold defeats Hardrada
1086Domesday Book — comprehensive land survey
📖 Key Terms
ProvenanceHousecarlsFyrdBayeux TapestryDomesday BookMotteBaileyFeudal SystemWACA
💡 Exam Tips
💡 WACA the Bayeux Tapestry — commissioned by Bishop Odo (William's half-brother). Does this matter?
💡 The Domesday Book shows the Conquest was an ADMINISTRATIVE revolution, not just military.
💡 Harold's three problems: forced march, tired army, died early.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe the events of the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066. Include: the positions of both armies, the key turning point, and what happened to Harold Godwinson.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why William won the Battle of Hastings in 1066. You should consider at least TWO reasons and use specific evidence.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'Harold Godwinson's own mistakes were the most important reason why the Norman Conquest was successful.' How far do you agree? Consider: Harold's mistakes, William's strengths, and the role of luck.

World War One 1914–18

📅 Key Dates
28 Jun 1914Franz Ferdinand assassinated at Sarajevo
1 Jul 1916Battle of the Somme — 57,470 British casualties on day one
11 Nov 1918Armistice
Jun 1919Treaty of Versailles — £6.6bn reparations
📖 Key Terms
M.A.I.N.MilitarismAlliancesImperialismNationalismSchlieffen PlanStalemateArmisticeReparations
💡 Exam Tips
💡 M.A.I.N. = Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism — UNDERLYING causes, not the spark.
💡 WACA both trench photographs: official IWM photographs — who took them? For whom? What purpose?
💡 Versailles imposed £6.6bn reparations — many historians link this to the rise of Hitler.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe the conditions experienced by British soldiers in the trenches during World War One.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain the M.A.I.N. causes of World War One. For EACH cause: define it, give ONE specific dated example, and explain HOW it contributed to the outbreak of war.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'The assassination of Franz Ferdinand at Sarajevo was the most important cause of World War One.' How far do you agree?

Was World War One caused more by long-term tensions than by the Sarajevo assassination?

The League of Nations

📅 Key Dates
Jan 1920League of Nations formally founded
1931Japan invades Manchuria — League takes no effective action
Oct 1935Italy invades Abyssinia — sanctions exclude oil
Jun 1936Haile Selassie addresses the League
📖 Key Terms
Collective SecurityCovenantSanctionsUnanimityAbyssiniaManchuriaAppeasement
💡 Exam Tips
💡 USA never joined — the Senate refused to ratify the Treaty of Versailles by 49 votes to 35.
💡 League had NO ARMY — could only impose economic sanctions which aggressors could ignore.
💡 WACA the Haile Selassie photograph: who took it? For whom? What was his aim?
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe the failure of the League of Nations in response to the Italian invasion of Abyssinia 1935–36.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain TWO structural weaknesses of the League of Nations. For each: what it was AND what specific consequence it had.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'The USA's decision not to join the League of Nations was the most important reason for its failure.' How far do you agree?

Cold War 1943–72

📅 Key Dates
Mar 1946Churchill's Iron Curtain speech
Mar 1947Truman Doctrine
Jun 1948Berlin Blockade begins
Oct 1962Cuban Missile Crisis — 13 days
1972SALT I Treaty
📖 Key Terms
Iron CurtainContainmentTruman DoctrineMarshall PlanBerlin BlockadeNATOWarsaw PactMADDétenteSALT ILOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
💡 Exam Tips
💡 LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE: Look → Check (WACA) → Infer → Judge — use on EVERY source question.
💡 8-mark source: WACA + specific quotes + own knowledge. ALL THREE required.
💡 Cuba: Kennedy chose BLOCKADE over air strike — know WHY this is the key 16-mark judgement.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why the Berlin Blockade (June 1948–May 1949) was a significant turning point in the Cold War. Use specific evidence.
Source (8 marks) — Iron Curtain Speech
How useful is Churchill's Iron Curtain Speech for understanding the origins of the Cold War? Para 1: content + 2 direct quotes. Para 2: WACA provenance (note Truman's presence). Para 3: own knowledge.
Essay (16 marks)
'The USA was more responsible than the USSR for Cold War tensions.' Para 1 (USA) → Para 2 (USSR) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Revision
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was the USA or the USSR more responsible for the tensions that led to the Cold War?

Stalin's Russia 1924–53

📅 Key Dates
1924–29Stalin consolidates power — outmanoeuvres Trotsky
1928First Five-Year Plan launched
1932–33Holodomor — 3.5–7.5 million die in Ukraine
1936–38Great Terror — Show Trials, mass arrests, Gulag expands
📖 Key Terms
Power StruggleFive-Year PlanCollectivisationKulaksHolodomorGreat TerrorShow TrialsNKVDGulagCult of PersonalityPropaganda
💡 Exam Tips
💡 Cover BOTH terror (NKVD, Gulag) AND propaganda (cult, censorship) — both required for full marks.
💡 Apply LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE to Stalin's official portrait: what visual choices communicate power?
💡 Show Trials: explain WHY defendants confessed — torture, family threats, propaganda.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain how Stalin maintained power through propaganda. Use specific evidence: cult of personality, censorship, Stakhanovites, and the role of the official portrait.
Source (8 marks) — Stalin's Portrait
How useful is Stalin's official portrait for understanding how Stalin maintained power? Para 1: LOOK. Para 2: CHECK (WACA). Para 3: own knowledge about what the portrait DOESN'T show.
Essay (16 marks)
'Terror was more important than propaganda in maintaining Stalin's control.' Para 1 (terror) → Para 2 (propaganda) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Revision
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was terror more important than propaganda in maintaining Stalin's power?

Vietnam War 1954–75

📅 Key Dates
May 1954Dien Bien Phu — France defeated
Aug 1964Gulf of Tonkin — US escalation
Jan 1968Tet Offensive — 100+ cities attacked
30 Apr 1975Fall of Saigon
📖 Key Terms
Viet MinhVietcongHo Chi MinhDomino TheoryGulf of TonkinSearch and DestroyCu Chi TunnelsTet OffensiveMy LaiVietnamisation
💡 Exam Tips
💡 TET OFFENSIVE: military = US WON. Political = US public confidence shattered. BOTH parts needed.
💡 Why did USA fail? MULTIPLE factors — Vietcong tactics, US tactics, home front, nationalism.
💡 16-mark: 'How far was US military tactics the main reason?' — always challenge with alternatives.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why the Vietcong's tactics were so effective against US forces. Use specific evidence: Cu Chi tunnels, hit-and-run raids, booby traps, and blending with civilians.
Source (8 marks) — Vietnam Composite
How useful is the Vietnam War composite image for understanding why the USA failed? Para 1: LOOK. Para 2: CHECK. Para 3: own knowledge.
Essay (16 marks)
'The Tet Offensive was the most important reason why the USA failed in Vietnam.' Para 1 (Tet: military vs political) → Para 2 (other factors) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Revision
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was the Tet Offensive the turning point of the Vietnam War — or just the moment the truth became visible?

Changes in Medicine c.1848–1948

📅 Key Dates
1928Fleming discovers penicillin — BEFORE WW2
1940Florey and Chain develop usable antibiotic — 12-year gap matters
Nov 1942Beveridge Report — Five Giants identified
5 Jul 1948NHS launches
📖 Key Terms
PenicillinFlemingFloreyChainBlood PlasmaGuinea Pig ClubBeveridge ReportWantDiseaseIgnoranceSqualorIdlenessNHSNye BevanBMA
💡 Exam Tips
💡 Apply LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE to the penicillin image — the 12-year gap between discovery (1928) and antibiotic (1940) is KEY.
💡 BMA opposed NHS — Bevan overcame them by giving consultants private patient rights.
💡 Name ALL FIVE Giants exactly: Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor, Idleness.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain the significance of penicillin in the history of medicine. Use specific evidence: Fleming's 1928 discovery, Florey and Chain's 1940 development, and its use in WW2. Note the 12-year gap.
Source (8 marks) — Bevan's Speech
How useful is Bevan's 1948 NHS launch speech for understanding the creation of the NHS? Para 1: content + 2 direct quotes. Para 2: WACA provenance. Para 3: own knowledge.
Essay (16 marks)
'WW2 was the main reason the NHS was created in 1948.' Para 1 (WW2 advances) → Para 2 (other factors: Beveridge, Labour 1945) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠

Blog Spot — Revision
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was World War Two the MAIN reason the NHS was created in 1948, or just an accelerator?

Cycle through 6, 8 and 16-mark questions. Write or speak your answer, then click Get Feedback for personalised AI guidance.

📘 Paper 1 — Cold War & Stalin
📝 Mark scheme tip

🎯 RAG Self-Assessment

Track your confidence with every learning statement across all topics. Click 🔴 (not yet), 🟠 (partly there) or 🟢 (confident). Your progress is saved automatically in your browser.

📅 Year 7
⚓ Year 8
🌍 Year 9
🎓 iGCSE

📅 Y7 — What is History & 1066

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — Chronology & Sources
📚 chronology · BC/AD · decade · century · millennium · source · evidence · primary · secondary
place events on a scaled timeline, use BC/AD vocabulary accurately and explain why chronology matters.
sort sources into primary/secondary and use provenance to judge how reliable a source is.
L2 — A History Dinner Party
📚 significance · interaction · era · civilisation
name six historical figures from different eras and cultures and explain what each is famous for.
explain how two contrasting guests might interact, with clear evidence-based reasons for every invitation.
L3 — Fact, Opinion, Cause & Effect
📚 fact · opinion · cause · effect · evidence · prove
sort historical statements into fact and opinion and give at least two historical cause-and-effect examples.
L4 — Primary & Secondary Sources
📚 primary · secondary · provenance · bias · artefact · reliable
define both types, give two examples of each and explain why provenance affects reliability.
L5 — What Happened in 1066?
📚 contender · Witan · oath · infer · chronicle · motte · bailey
describe all four claimants and the strength of each claim; use four Anglo-Saxon sources to infer what England was like.
rank the claimants from strongest to weakest and justify my ranking with evidence.
L6 — The Battle of Hastings
📚 housecarl · fyrd · shield wall · feigned retreat · Senlac Hill
sequence events from dawn to Harold's death and explain three reasons why William won and Harold lost.
L7 — How Did Harold Die?
📚 Bayeux Tapestry · bias · conflicting sources · provenance
identify how sources disagree about Harold's death and explain why — using the concept of bias.
weigh sources by who made them, when and why, then reach a supported written judgement.
L8 — What Happened After Hastings?
📚 conquest · garrison · coronation · submit · ravage · Domesday
explain why William could not march straight to London and judge why he became king so quickly.
⭐ One thing I am proud of in this unit:
🔴 Red = Revisit │ 🟠 Amber = Almost there │ 🟢 Green = Confident │ HPL (grey): Analysing · Creating · Linking · Meta-thinking · Realising · Empathy · Hard-working · Agile │ AI Literacy (blue): AI Concepts · Data Literacy · Critical Evaluation · Ethics & AI

🏰 Y7 — Castles Project

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
Location — Where to Build a Castle
📚 high ground · natural moat · visibility · supply route · Maintown sites A–E
explain four features of a good site and judge which of the five Maintown sites is strongest — with evidence.
Motte & Bailey Castles
📚 motte · bailey · palisade · drawbridge · keep · 7–14 days · 1066
label all key parts of a motte & bailey castle and explain why 100+ were built so quickly after 1066.
explain why timber was replaced by stone from c.1070 and what this tells us about Norman power.
How Castles Developed
📚 stone keep · concentric castle · round tower · Crusades · gatehouse · gunpowder
describe the four design stages and explain how Crusader ideas influenced the concentric castle.
Attacking & Defending — Siege Warfare
📚 trebuchet · mangonel · battering ram · sappers · undermining · Rochester 1215
describe three attack weapons and three defensive features, using the Siege of Rochester (1215) as a specific example.
Castle Life & Decline
📚 lord · constable · steward · gong farmer · gunpowder · peace · comfort
name five castle roles and explain four reasons why castles declined after c.1400.
Research Skills & Project Quality
📚 paraphrase · plagiarism · heading · primary source · ethics · reliability
research without copying, credit sources, and structure my project with headings and evidence (at least Silver level).
critically evaluate online sources and AI suggestions, asking who made them, when and why.
⭐ One thing I am proud of in this unit:
🔴 Red = Revisit │ 🟠 Amber = Almost there │ 🟢 Green = Confident │ HPL (grey): Analysing · Creating · Linking · Meta-thinking · Realising · Empathy · Hard-working · Agile │ AI Literacy (blue): AI Concepts · Data Literacy · Critical Evaluation · Ethics & AI

🎉 Y7 — Medieval Entertainment

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — Could You Have Fun? Pastimes & Holy Days
📚 holy day · tournament · mob football · stoolball · conkers · bowling · ice skating
sort five medieval pastimes into rich/noble and ordinary people's activities and explain which survive today.
explain why one pastime died out (e.g. mob football banned for violence) and how a surviving one has changed.
L2 — Football Through the Ages
📚 mob football · royal decree · ban · Source A 1174 · Source B 1314
use both sources to explain why mob football was enjoyed AND banned, then compare it with modern football.
judge — using evidence — whether mob football was more like a sport or a riot.
L3 — Let Me Entertain You (Music, Minstrels & Plays)
📚 minstrel · miracle play · sacred music · house music · lute · feast
explain the difference between sacred and house music, and judge whether music was mainly entertainment or education.
L4 — Keeping in Fashion (Clothes & Status)
📚 status symbol · poulaines · steeple hat · pattens · natural dye
explain how colours and materials showed a person's rank, using source evidence about rich and peasant clothing.
L5 — The Story of English
📚 Old English · Norse · Latin · French · Chaucer · Middle English
explain why French became dominant after 1066, how English survived, and give word examples from three languages.
L6 — Come Dine With Me! (Medieval Food & Feasts)
📚 trencher · pottage · noble · feast · ale · peasant diet · salted meat
describe a peasant's meals, explain why ale was safer than water and compare peasant vs noble diets.
judge whether the medieval feast shows fairness or inequality, and use AI to research food facts then spot anachronisms.
⭐ One thing I am proud of in this unit:

☠️ Y7 — Black Death

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — Experiencing the Black Death
📚 plague · symptom · bubo · mortality · outbreak · victim
identify four symptoms from source accounts using accurate vocabulary, and explain why people were so terrified.
L2 — Causes & Cures
📚 miasma · flagellant · scapegoat · astrology · quarantine · contagion
classify explanations as religious or natural, evaluate which came closest to the real cause, and explain the danger of scapegoating.
L3 — How Did the Black Death Spread?
📚 trade route · port · flea · rat · chronicle · contamination
track the plague's route into England using maps/sources and explain three reasons it spread so fast.
L4 — Medieval Doctors & Healers
📚 physician · barber-surgeon · apothecary · uroscopy · four humours · diagnosis
name and compare all three types of healer, describe their methods and say which social group could afford each.
L5 — Terrifying Treatments & Herbal Remedies
📚 bloodletting · purging · trepanning · garlic · willow · honey
sort treatments by risk level and explain which could genuinely help (willow = aspirin, honey = antiseptic).
judge which treatment was most harmful and which had the best chance of helping — with reasons.
L6 — Was It Dangerous to Be Rich?
📚 dysentery · gout · Henry I · King John · Edward I · pattern
use the deaths of three kings to explain hidden health risks for the rich, and judge whether wealth protected people.
⭐ One thing I am proud of in this unit:

💣 Y9 — World War One

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — The World in 1901
📚 empire · rivalry · arms race · working class · standard of living
name the great powers, describe Britain's "two nations" and rank six inventions by their impact on daily life.
L2 — The Causes of War (M.A.I.N.)
📚 Militarism · Alliances · Imperialism · Nationalism · Sarajevo · Franz Ferdinand
define all four M.A.I.N. causes, describe the Sarajevo spark and sequence the six-week chain reaction.
judge which long-term cause was most important and distinguish it clearly from the short-term trigger.
L3 — The Course of the War (Trenches)
📚 trench · parapet · fire step · No Man's Land · stalemate · Somme · Passchendaele
label trench parts, place five key battles on a timeline and explain why the war became a deadly stalemate.
judge whether Haig deserved the nickname "Butcher" using evidence from both the Somme and Passchendaele.
L4 — Weapons & Medicine
📚 machine gun · poison gas · tank · triage · blood transfusion · X-ray · shell shock · Gillies
rank WW1 weapons from deadliest to least and explain how triage, transfusion and X-rays saved lives.
explain Gillies's tubed pedicle technique and why he is called the "father of plastic surgery".
L5 — How the War Ended
📚 Armistice · B-R-A-T · reparations · War Guilt Clause · Big Four · Diktat
describe Versailles terms using B-R-A-T, explain why Germany called it a Diktat, and judge whether it was fair.
L6 — Remembrance & Legacy
📚 poppy · commemoration · legacy · PEEL · "war to end all wars"
explain the poppy's symbolism, identify three ways WW1 changed the world, and write a PEEL paragraph on the most important cause.
⭐ One thing I am proud of in this unit:

⛵ Y9 — Nelson & Trafalgar

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — Life at Sea
📚 press gang · grog · scurvy · flogging · hammock · ship of the line · rations
describe daily life at sea (food, discipline, the watch, press gang) and explain how each shaped a sailor's life.
judge whether food, disease or discipline was the greatest threat to a sailor's life, using source evidence.
L2 — Nelson's Career
📚 Nelson Touch · Band of Brothers · Nile 1798 · Copenhagen 1801 · blind eye · initiative
describe the Nile and Copenhagen victories, explain the "Nelson Touch" and why it earned total loyalty.
judge whether Nelson's tactical skill or personal courage mattered more to his success.
L3 — The Battle of Trafalgar, 21 October 1805
📚 HMS Victory · two columns · breaking the line · broadside · "England expects…"
describe the two-column plan, sequence key events and explain two reasons Britain won.
judge how decisive Nelson's personal leadership was, using the Death of Nelson engraving as source evidence.
L4 — End-of-Unit Assessment (Battle Guide or Essay)
📚 PEEL · consolidate · significance · judgement · evidence · audience
select relevant facts from all three lessons, structure work clearly and use PEEL to build a supported argument.
reach a supported verdict on "Who or what won victory at Trafalgar?" — weighing leadership, tactics and luck.

🌍 Y8 — British Empire

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — The Growth of the British Empire
📚 empire · raw materials · markets · power & prestige · duty · war · settlers · trade
identify four reasons Britain wanted an empire and four methods used to build it.
judge which reason was most important and explain why India became the empire's greatest prize.
L2 — India Before the British
📚 Mughals · jewel in the crown · cotton · spices · Taj Mahal · Golden Temple
describe Mughal India's cities, wealth and learning, then judge how accurate the phrase "jewel in the crown" is.
L3 — Invasion — the East India Company
📚 EIC · sepoy · Battle of Plassey 1757 · Robert Clive · tax · Bengal
explain how the EIC built an army, won at Plassey (1757) and turned trade into conquest in three steps.
judge: how was a private trading company able to conquer an entire country?
L4 — The Indian Mutiny, 1857
📚 greased cartridges · Doctrine of Lapse · viceroy · Raj · nationalism · sepoy
explain the cartridge trigger and three long-term causes, then describe the consequences (Raj, Viceroy, nationalism).
judge whether the spark or the deeper grievances were the most important cause of the Mutiny.
L5 — The Jewel in the Crown (The British Raj)
📚 British Raj · Viceroy · Empress of India 1877 · Curzon · Gandhi · Nehru
describe Raj governance, weigh positive and negative effects, and use Curzon's, Gandhi's and Nehru's viewpoints.
judge: on balance, did the British Raj help or harm India more? Justify my verdict with evidence.

🕊️ Y9 — League of Nations

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1–2 — Why Was the League Set Up?
📚 League of Nations · Treaty of Versailles · covenant · self-determination · disarmament · collective
explain the four aims, their origins in Wilson's Fourteen Points, and judge which aim mattered most.
use the cartoon ark source to explain people's hopes for the League after WW1.
L3–4 — How Did the League Work?
📚 Assembly · Council · Secretariat · sanctions · veto · unanimous · "Gap in the Bridge"
describe the three bodies and four peacekeeping tools; explain why no army and missing USA were fundamental weaknesses.
evaluate which weakness hurt the League most: no army, missing members or the need for unanimous decisions.
L5–6 — Did the League Work in the 1920s?
📚 Aaland Islands · Corfu Incident · ILO · Nansen passport · humanitarian · aggressor
list four 1920s successes and explain the League's humanitarian work (ILO, refugees, Slavery Commission).
use the Corfu Incident (1923) to explain why the League failed when a great power was involved.
L7–8 — Why Did the League Fail in the 1930s?
📚 Great Depression · Manchuria 1931 · Lytton Report · Abyssinia 1935 · Hoare–Laval Pact · appeasement
explain how Manchuria (1931) and Abyssinia (1935) each destroyed the League's credibility and authority.
rank the reasons for failure and design a satirical cartoon giving my verdict on the League.

🏙️ Y8 — Living Conditions

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1–2 — Filth & Disease: Living Conditions
📚 sewage · contaminated · epidemic · back-to-back housing · cholera · public health
identify key features of Victorian living conditions and explain how dirty water/waste spread disease.
write a health inspector's report identifying hazards and judge which problem was the most serious.
L3–4 — How Did Disease Spread So Fast?
📚 typhoid · tuberculosis · miasma · waterborne · airborne · germ theory
compare how cholera, typhoid and TB each spread; explain why Victorians blamed miasma instead of germs.
judge which disease posed the greatest threat, using speed of death, victims affected and public fear.
L5–6 — Why Was the Government's Response So Slow?
📚 Chadwick · Public Health Act 1848 · laissez-faire · Source A (cholera notice) · Source B (report)
explain three reasons the government was slow to act and why the 1848 Act was insufficient (it was optional).
judge who drove change most: government pressure, scientific evidence or public fear.
L7–8 — Public Health Heroes
📚 Chadwick · John Snow · Bazalgette · Nightingale · Broad Street pump · sewer network
describe what each of the four reformers did and explain how Snow proved cholera was waterborne.
judge which reformer was most significant and rebut a counter-argument for one of the others.

🏭 Y8 — Farms to Factories

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — How Britain Changed 1750–1900
📚 Industrial Revolution · significance · population · transport · railways · culture · politics
give examples of change in all six areas (population, work, culture, health, transport, politics) comparing 1750 with 1900.
judge which change was the most significant and justify my answer with evidence.
L2 — Homeworkers to Factory Workers
📚 domestic system · factory system · Spinning Jenny · flying shuttle · mass production
explain how the domestic system worked and why machines moved production into factories.
compare home working with factory working using three differences and judge if the move was good for workers.
L3 — How Factories Created Towns
📚 overcrowded · pollution · services · railway · canal · Bradford · urbanisation
explain rapid urban growth (Bradford: 13,000→103,000 by 1851), two good and two bad effects.
judge whether factories made towns better or worse places to live, using source evidence.
L4 — Conditions & Victorian Inventors
📚 Sadler Report 1832 · Factory Act · pauper apprentice · Stephenson · Brunel · Nightingale · Faraday
describe factory conditions using Peggy the Pauper and explain how the Sadler Report led to the Factory Acts.
explain my chosen inventor's significance then and now, and judge which inventor was most significant overall.

⛓️ Y8 — Slavery

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
L1 — West Africa Before Slavery
📚 Ghana · Mali · Songhay · Benin · Timbuktu · gold · salt trade
name three West African kingdoms, describe their power and wealth, and explain how this challenges racist myths about Africa.
L2 — The Triangular Trade
📚 triangular trade · Middle Passage · plantation · profit · Liverpool · Bristol
describe the three trade stages, explain why it was so profitable, and judge who was most to blame.
L3 — The Middle Passage
📚 slave ship · chains · dysentery · Olaudah Equiano · one in five
describe conditions on a slave ship and explain why ≈ one in five died; write a diary entry using Equiano's account.
L4 — Life on the Plantations
📚 auction · scramble · branded · overseer · rebellion · Maroons · resistance
describe how enslaved people were bought, sold and worked; explain three forms of resistance and judge which was most powerful.
L5 — Abolition & Abolitionists
📚 Wilberforce · Clarkson · Sharp · Equiano · petition · sugar boycott · 1807 · 1833
explain two abolition factors, describe Equiano's and the boycott's role in changing public opinion.
judge which factor was most important in ending slavery and justify my answer with evidence.
L6 — Slavery Today
📚 modern slavery · human trafficking · forced labour · exploitation · Anti-Slavery International
describe two forms of modern slavery, explain where and why it happens, and judge how much has changed since 1833.
L7 — Who Was to Blame?
📚 blame · responsibility · profit · complicity · capitalist · banker · plantation owner
rank eight groups from most to least to blame, justify my ranking and write a persuasive abolitionist speech.

🏥 iGCSE — Changes in Medicine

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
Unit 1 (Wks 1–2) — Old Ideas + Care, Surgery & Reform (1848–60)
📚 Four Humours · Miasma (bad air causes disease) · Spontaneous generation · Public Health Act 1848 (op
describe the Four Humours, miasma and spontaneous generation, and explain why the 1848 Public Health Act did little (it was optional, so most councils ignored it).
describe Nightingale's Scutari reforms, Simpson's chloroform (1847) and Snow's Broad Street pump (1854), then judge which was the biggest improvement before 1860.
Unit 2 (Wks 3–4) — Germ Theory & Antiseptics (1861–75)
📚 Pasteur: Germ Theory 1861 · Swan-neck flask (microbes in air cause decay; disproved spontaneous gene
describe Pasteur's swan-neck flask experiment step by step and state exactly what it proved (microbes cause decay) and disproved (spontaneous generation).
explain how Lister's carbolic acid (from 1867) cut surgical deaths and how the 1875 Act (compulsory, not optional like 1848) transformed public health.
Unit 3 (Wks 5–6) — Koch, Ehrlich & Curie (1875–1905)
📚 Koch: TB bacillus 1882 · cholera 1883 · agar plates · Petri dish · staining dyes · Ehrlich: Salvarsa
describe Koch's three laboratory methods (staining, agar, Petri dish) and name his two bacteria (TB 1882, cholera 1883), then explain how Ehrlich's Salvarsan 606 (1909) cured syphilis as the first magic bullet.
explain how Curie's mobile X-ray vans helped locate shrapnel, and judge which of Koch, Ehrlich or Curie made the most significant advance in 1875–1905.
Unit 4 (Wk 7) — Medicine & the Trenches (1914–18)
📚 RAMC · Triage · Sodium citrate 1914 (stopped clotting) · Blood depots Cambrai 1917 · Wound excision
explain how sodium citrate (1914) and refrigerated blood depots at Cambrai (1917) made large-scale transfusion possible, then describe Gillies' tubed pedicle skin-graft technique at Sidcup.
judge which WW1 advance saved the most lives — blood transfusion, Gillies' plastic surgery or Curie's X-ray vans — using evidence on impact and medical legacy.
Unit 5 (Wk 8) — Penicillin & Mass Production
📚 Fleming 1928: Penicillium notatum at St Mary's — no funding, unstable, could not purify · Florey & C
explain why Fleming's 1928 discovery did not produce a drug (no funding; unstable; could not purify), and how Florey & Chain purified and tested it on a human patient in 1941.
explain why US deep-fermentation tanks (1943) were essential for mass production, and judge whether penicillin was the most important development across the whole period 1848–1948.
Unit 6 (Wk 9) — War, Welfare & the NHS (1939–48)
📚 McIndoe: saline baths (replaced tannic acid) · Guinea Pig Club · East Grinstead · Dr Charles Drew: d
describe McIndoe's saline-bath burns treatment, the Guinea Pig Club, and Dr Drew's dried-plasma programme — then list Beveridge's five giant evils from his 1942 report.
explain how Beveridge's five giants led Bevan to launch the NHS on 5 July 1948, and judge whether this was the most important turning point in British medical history 1848–1948.
📝 Exam Skills — Edexcel iGCSE History
6-Mark Question
• Describe / explain in detail
• Two features or points required
Level 1 1–5 marks
• General statements, limited knowledge, no developed explanation.
answer a 6-mark question by describing two features/points in detail, each supported by precise factual evidence (dates, names, statistics).
answer an 8-mark question by explaining two fully developed reasons or effects, using a chain of reasoning with 'this meant…' / 'as a result…' connectives.
plan a 16-mark 'How far do you agree?' answer with two arguments on each side, reach a supported judgement and write at Level 4 standard.
apply LOOK · PROVENANCE · INFER · JUDGE to any source and use it as evidence — correctly citing the source and its attribution in my answer.
⭐ One thing I am most confident about:

☭ iGCSE — Cold War

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
Lesson 1 — Reasons for the Cold War
📚 Capitalism · Communism · Ideology · Grand Alliance · Big Three (Roosevelt, Churchill, Stalin) · Tehr
contrast capitalism and communism, profile the Big Three's aims at Tehran, Yalta and Potsdam, and apply LOOK–PROVENANCE–INFER–JUDGE to the Yalta photograph.
explain how the atomic bomb (dropped without warning Stalin, Aug 1945) and leadership changes at Potsdam deepened mistrust, and judge whether ideology or the conferences mattered more.
Lesson 2 — Early Developments, 1945–49
📚 Iron Curtain · Satellite states · Salami tactics · Truman Doctrine $400m (Mar 1947) · Marshall Plan
explain salami tactics, how the Truman Doctrine and Marshall Plan practised containment, and sequence the Berlin Blockade/Airlift (Jun 1948–May 1949: peak one plane every 90 seconds; ~2.3m tonnes).
judge whether the Berlin Blockade was a victory or defeat for Stalin, and explain how it led to the FRG, GDR and NATO by 1949.
Lesson 3 — Superpower Rivalry, 1950–56
📚 Korean War 1950–53 (38th parallel; ~3m dead; China enters) · Arms race: USSR atomic bomb 1949 · Dete
explain the Korean War as containment in action and describe the logic of deterrence — MAD meant direct war was too dangerous to risk.
explain why the USSR crushed the Hungarian Uprising (4 Nov 1956 — Nagy threatened to leave the Warsaw Pact; ~20,000 killed) and judge whether peaceful co-existence was real or just a pause.
Lesson 4 — Three Crises: Berlin, Cuba & Czechoslovakia
📚 Berlin Wall Aug 1961 (3m fled; Checkpoint Charlie Oct 1961) · Cuban Missile Crisis 14–28 Oct 1962 (U
sequence the 13 days of the Cuban Missile Crisis (U-2 photos → quarantine → ships turn back → Black Saturday → withdrawal) and explain why Kennedy chose a blockade over an air strike.
rank the three crises (Berlin Wall, Cuba, Prague Spring) from most to least dangerous and judge which brought the world closest to nuclear war — with precise evidence.
Lesson 5 — The Thaw & Détente, 1963–72
📚 Hotline 1963 · Test Ban Treaty 1963 (no atmospheric/space/underwater tests) · Outer Space Treaty 196
compare the four arms-control agreements (Hotline, Test Ban, Outer Space Treaty, NPT) and explain why the USA, USSR and China each had its own reasons for wanting détente.
judge whether SALT 1 (1972) was a genuine turning point — achievements (first missile limit; Nixon in Moscow) vs limits (capped not cut; mistrust remained).

🎖️ iGCSE — Stalin's Russia

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
Unit 1 — The Struggle for Power, 1924–29
📚 Politburo · Lenin's Testament ('Stalin is too rude') · General Secretary · Left Opposition (Trotsky,
name all five contenders (Trotsky, Zinoviev, Kamenev, Bukharin, Stalin) with one strength and one weakness each, and explain why the Politburo suppressed Lenin's Testament in May 1924.
sequence Stalin's defeat of the Left (1924–27: Trotsky lost Red Army 1925; United Opposition crushed 1927; Trotsky exiled 1929) then the Right (1929: Bukharin beaten), and judge whether his General Secretary post or his rivals' mistakes was more decisive.
Unit 2 — Modernising the Economy, 1928–41
📚 Command economy · Gosplan · Three Five-Year Plans · Stalin 1931: 'We are 50 years behind… or they wi
describe the aims and methods of the Five-Year Plans and use real output data (coal 35→150 mt; steel 4→18 mt; electricity 5→48 bn kWh) as evidence of industrial achievement.
explain how collectivisation caused the 1932–33 Holodomor (4–5m dead; grain seized for export while villages starved; cattle 70→38m) and judge whether the Plans' achievements outweighed the human cost.
Unit 3 — Terror, Purges & Propaganda, 1934–38
📚 NKVD · Kirov murder Dec 1934 · Yezhovshchina · Show trials: 1936 the Sixteen (Zinoviev/Kamenev); 193
name the Kirov trigger (Dec 1934), list all three Moscow show trials and their victims, and use scale data (7–8m arrested; 1–1.5m executed; ~2m Gulag deaths) to describe the human cost of the Terror.
explain how the cult (portraits, rewritten history, Vyshinsky's 'mad dogs must be shot') and terror worked together, and judge whether fear alone explains how Stalin kept power after 1938.
Unit 4 — Life in the Soviet Union, 1924–41
📚 1940 Labour Code · Zhenotdel (closed 1930) · Family Code 1936 (banned abortion) · Double burden · Li
use data (literacy 55→94%; 13m women in workforce; Pasha Angelina; Moscow 2.2→4.1m by 1936) to describe changes for workers and women, and explain the 'double burden' and 1936 Family Code.
judge — using Stalin's Nov 1935 'life has become more joyous' speech against housing (~4 sq m), rationing (to 1935), the 1940 Labour Code and Korean deportations — whose lives improved and whose got worse before 1941.
Unit 5 — The Second World War & After, 1941–53
📚 Barbarossa 22 Jun 1941 · Scorched earth · Great Patriotic War · Tankograd (Chelyabinsk) · T-34 · Len
explain the impact of Barbarossa (22 Jun 1941), how 1,500+ factories moved east and Tankograd out-produced Germany in T-34 tanks by 1943, then trace Stalingrad (Operation Uranus Nov 1942 — 300,000+ encircled) as the turning point.
judge Stalin's legacy — moderniser (industrial output, Stalingrad, superpower) vs tyrant (~27m war dead, Holodomor, Gulag, Leningrad Affair 1949) — and reach a supported verdict.

🌏 iGCSE — Vietnam War

Click 🔴 🟠 🟢 for each learning statement. Your progress is saved automatically.
Learning Statement 🔴 Not yet 🟠 Partly 🟢 Got it!
Unit 1 — Origins & the Viet Minh (Week 1)
📚 Indochina (France from 1880s) · Japanese occupation from 1940 · Famine 1944–45 (~2m dead) · Ho Chi M
explain how Japanese occupation and the 1944–45 famine (~2m dead) weakened French control and gave Ho Chi Minh's Viet Minh the opportunity to declare independence on 2 Sep 1945.
Unit 2 — Colonial Vietnam & the French War, 1946–54 (Week 2)
📚 First Indochina War 1946–54 · Dien Bien Phu: Giap besieged French in valley · Chinese artillery haul
explain how Giap besieged the French in the Dien Bien Phu valley using Chinese artillery, cite de Castries's radio message, and describe what the 1954 Geneva Accords decided.
Unit 3 — General Giap, the "Red Napoleon" (Week 3)
📚 Born 1911 Quang Binh · Hanoi University · Communist Party 1930 · NVA commander-in-chief · Guerrilla
give two specific examples of why Giap deserves the title 'Red Napoleon' — siege tactics at Dien Bien Phu and Ho Chi Minh Trail attrition against the USA — with precise evidence.
Unit 4 — US Involvement & Escalation (Week 4)
📚 Domino theory · Ngo Dinh Diem (corrupt; strategic hamlets) · Viet Cong / NLF from 1960 · Gulf of Ton
explain how the domino theory, Diem's unpopular regime, the Viet Cong insurgency and the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (Aug 1964) combined to take the USA into full-scale war.
Unit 5 — Fighting the War (Week 5)
📚 Cu Chi tunnels · Ambush · Booby traps · Ho Chi Minh Trail (Laos & Cambodia) · Rolling Thunder 1965 ·
compare Viet Cong tactics (Cu Chi tunnels, ambush, attrition via Trail) with US methods (Rolling Thunder, search-and-destroy, Agent Orange, Phoenix Programme), and explain why superior US firepower still failed.
Unit 6 — War's End & Legacy (Week 6)
📚 Tet Offensive Jan 1968 (100+ towns; psychological shock) · My Lai massacre Mar 1968 · Kent State 4 M
explain how Tet (Jan 1968), My Lai and Kent State (May 1970) forced Nixon's Vietnamisation policy, and judge whether Tet was the main turning point of the war.
write a 16-mark answer on the effects of the war — weighing the human cost in Vietnam (1–4m civilians; 2m refugees) against the political cost in the USA — at Level 4 standard.
Unit 7 — Ho Chi Minh (Week 7)
📚 Born 1890 Nghe An · French Communist Party 1920 · Viet Minh 1941 · DRV President 2 Sep 1945 · Simult
use key events (FCP 1920; Viet Minh 1941; independence 2 Sep 1945) to argue Ho Chi Minh was simultaneously a nationalist AND a communist, and judge which belief drove him more.
Unit 8 — Vietcong Guerrilla Tactics (Week 8)
📚 Guerrilla warfare · Ambush · Booby traps (punji traps) · Cu Chi tunnels (near Saigon) · Night attack
map and rank the Vietcong's top three tactics (tunnels, ambush, attrition/Tet 1968), explain how each neutralised US firepower, and judge which mattered most to the 1975 victory.

👆 Select a topic above to start your self-assessment
📚 Key Terms Glossary

All key vocabulary across Year 7 to iGCSE. Use the search box to find a term instantly.

📚 Year 7
BC / AD
BC = Before Christ (counts backwards). AD = Anno Domini (counts forwards from year 0).
Primary Source
Created at the time of the event, by someone who was there.
Secondary Source
Created after the event, using primary sources as evidence.
WACA
Who · Audience · Context · Aim — the framework for analysing historical sources.
Provenance
Information about where a source comes from — who made it, when, and why.
Chronology
Placing events in the correct time order.
Motte
The raised earthen mound topped with a wooden or stone tower in a Norman castle.
Bailey
The enclosed courtyard at the base of the motte, housing domestic buildings.
Siege
Surrounding and cutting off a fortification to force surrender by starvation or assault.
Trebuchet
A counterweight catapult used to hurl heavy projectiles at castle walls.
Bubonic Plague
Form of the Black Death spread by flea bites from infected rats. Caused buboes.
Miasma
The (wrong) medieval theory that disease was caused by poisonous 'bad air'.
Holodomor
The man-made famine in Ukraine 1932–33, killing 5–7 million people.
Minstrel
A travelling entertainer who told stories through music and song.
Sumptuary Laws
Laws restricting what people could wear based on their social class.
Feudal System
The medieval social hierarchy: king → barons → knights → peasants.
📚 Year 8
Triangular Trade
The three-sided trade route: Europe → Africa (enslaved people) → Americas (goods) → Europe.
Middle Passage
The horrific sea crossing of enslaved Africans from West Africa to the Americas.
Abolitionist
Someone who campaigned to end the slave trade and slavery.
Domestic System
Pre-industrial system where workers produced goods at home.
Industrial Revolution
The rapid shift from agricultural to industrial production in Britain c.1750–1850.
Cholera
A waterborne disease that killed thousands in Victorian Britain before sanitation reform.
Laissez-faire
The Victorian belief that government should not interfere in people's lives or markets.
Imperialism
The policy of extending a nation's power through colonisation or economic dominance.
Sepoy
An Indian soldier serving in the East India Company's army.
East India Company
A private British trading company that came to govern much of India.
Raj
British rule over India from 1858 to 1947.
Conscription
Compulsory military service introduced in Britain in January 1916.
Collectivisation
Stalin's policy of forcing peasants to work on state-owned collective farms.
📚 Year 9
Press Gang
A group that forcibly recruited men into the Royal Navy.
Nelson Touch
Nelson's leadership style: share the plan, trust initiative, care for men, calculated aggression.
MAIN
Militarism · Alliances · Imperialism · Nationalism — the four long-term causes of WW1.
Stalemate
A situation where neither side can make progress — characteristic of WW1's Western Front.
Trench Warfare
Fighting from defensive trenches — the dominant form of combat on the Western Front.
Armistice
An agreement to stop fighting — the WW1 armistice came into effect 11 November 1918 at 11am.
Collective Security
The idea that all League members would defend any member attacked — the League's core principle.
Sanctions
Economic or military penalties imposed on a country as punishment.
Appeasement
The policy of making concessions to an aggressive power to avoid war.
Treaty of Versailles
The 1919 peace treaty that ended WW1, imposing harsh terms on Germany.
📚 iGCSE
Containment
US policy of preventing the spread of communism beyond its existing borders.
Domino Theory
The belief that if one country fell to communism, its neighbours would follow.
Détente
A period of improved relations between the USA and USSR from the 1960s–70s.
Mutually Assured Destruction
The Cold War doctrine that nuclear war would destroy both sides, deterring first use.
Satellite State
A country that is officially independent but in practice controlled by a larger power.
Iron Curtain
Churchill's term for the division between communist Eastern Europe and the democratic West.
Show Trial
A public trial in which the verdict is decided in advance; confessions obtained by force.
Gulag
The Soviet system of forced labour camps. 18 million people passed through 1930–53.
Collectivisation
Stalin's policy forcing 25 million peasant households onto state-controlled collective farms.
Viet Cong
Communist guerrilla fighters operating in South Vietnam against US and ARVN forces.
Vietnamisation
Nixon's policy of gradually withdrawing US troops while building up South Vietnamese forces.
Germ Theory
Pasteur's 1861 discovery that specific microorganisms cause specific diseases.
Antiseptic
A substance that kills microorganisms to prevent infection — Lister used carbolic acid from 1867.
Penicillin
The world's first antibiotic, discovered by Fleming 1928, mass-produced from 1943.
NHS
National Health Service — established 1948, providing free healthcare for all in Britain.
Credibility Gap
The growing gulf between US government claims of Vietnam progress and the reality.
Tet Offensive
January 1968: coordinated Viet Cong attacks on 100+ South Vietnamese cities.

Y7
Bayeux Tapestry, c.1077
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Commissioned by Bishop Odo of Bayeux
[VISUAL SOURCE] A continuous narrative in 58 scenes: Harold's oath, death of Edward, Harold's coronation, William's invasion fleet, the Battle of Hastings, Harold's death, and William's victory.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who commissioned this tapestry — and why does knowing this change how we use it?
Consider: Commissioned by Bishop Odo — William's half-brother. Would Odo commission a tapestry showing the Normans negatively?
WACA Question 2
When was it made — and who actually made it?
Consider: Made in the 1070s, probably by Anglo-Saxon embroiderers working for a Norman patron. What does it mean that the MAKERS are different from the COMMISSIONER?
WACA Question 3
What is this source most useful for — and what can it NOT tell us?
Consider: The Tapestry shows clothing, weapons, ships and battle tactics. Does it show Harold's perspective fairly?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: Full WACA Analysis
  • Apply WACA fully: Who commissioned it? For what audience? In what context? With what aim?
  • Write your JUDGEMENT: 'This source is useful for understanding [X] because..., however it is limited because...'
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y7
Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, c.1100
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Written approximately 34 years after Hastings by monks
Harold was informed that William the Bastard had landed at Pevensey. Harold at once marched from the north. Many men fell ill on the march and many remained behind. William came by surprise against him before his army was set in order.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who wrote this source and how might their identity affect what they recorded?
Consider: Written by monks — educated men associated with the Church. Would monks be sympathetic to Harold (Anglo-Saxon) or William (Norman)?
WACA Question 2
When was this written — and why does the 34-year gap matter?
Consider: Written approximately 34 years after 1066. What might monks writing in 1100 know or not know?
WACA Question 3
What is this source most useful for — and what are its specific limitations?
Consider: What does this source tell us clearly? What does it NOT tell us?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: 8-Mark Practice
  • Question: 'How useful is the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle for understanding why Harold lost at Hastings?'
  • Para 1: content + 2 direct quotes. Para 2: WACA provenance. Para 3: own knowledge. Target 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y7
Danse Macabre, Wolgemut, c.1493
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Visual source — 145 years AFTER the Black Death
[VISUAL SOURCE] Skeletal figures of Death dancing with people from every social class — popes, kings, merchants, knights and peasants — all being led to their graves equally. No social rank escapes Death's summons.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
What specific visual choices has the artist made — and what message do they send?
Consider: Death takes everyone equally regardless of social rank. What does this tell us about how people in 1493 understood the Black Death?
WACA Question 2
When was this made — and why does the 145-year gap matter?
Consider: Made in 1493 — 145 years after the Black Death of 1348. The artist did NOT witness the plague.
WACA Question 3
How does this visual source complement John of Reading's written account?
Consider: John of Reading described symptoms. The Danse Macabre shows the EMOTIONAL response.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: List FIVE specific visual elements
  • CHECK (WACA): Who? When (1493 — 145 years after)? For whom? What aim?
  • JUDGE: How useful for understanding the Black Death of 1348? 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y8
Brookes Slave Ship Diagram, 1787
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Published by abolitionists
[VISUAL SOURCE] Cross-section diagram of the Brookes' lower decks, showing 482 enslaved people laid out in rows. Measurements show the height of spaces (50-55cm) and width per person (16 inches).
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who produced this — and what was its specific aim?
Consider: Produced by ABOLITIONISTS. Does knowing it is propaganda make it less useful? Or more useful as evidence of abolitionist methods?
WACA Question 2
What is this diagram designed to make viewers FEEL — and how does it achieve this?
Consider: The diagram uses precise measurements. Why use a TECHNICAL diagram rather than a painting?
WACA Question 3
How does this visual source complement Equiano's written account?
Consider: Equiano described what the Middle Passage FELT like. The Brookes shows what it looked like from OUTSIDE.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: Describe what you can see — layout, measurements, number of figures, labels
  • CHECK (WACA): Who? When? For whom? What abolitionist aim?
  • JUDGE: Write your judgement sentence. 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y9
British Soldier, Western Front, 1916 (IWM)
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Censored letter — military officers read all soldiers' post
We are now in the third week at the front. The mud is indescribable. Men have been pulled under by it entirely. Last night we lost two men who disappeared into shell holes. The noise never stops. I have not slept properly in eleven days. I think about home every moment.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who wrote this — and why does the CENSORSHIP of the letter matter?
Consider: The letter was censored — meaning the soldier knew it would be read by a military officer. How might this affect what he chose to write?
WACA Question 2
What does this source tell us — and what can it NOT tell us?
Consider: Excellent for showing individual human experience. But can it tell us about strategic decisions?
WACA Question 3
Compare with the trench photographs in your WW1 topic section.
Consider: The photographs show the physical environment; the letter shows the psychological experience.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: WACA — The Problem of Censorship
  • Apply WACA fully — particularly CONTEXT: who censored this letter? Why? What might be deliberately missing?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this soldier's letter for understanding the human cost of WW1?' 150-200 words.
🧠

Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?

Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y9
Haile Selassie, League of Nations, June 1936
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Ethiopia's appeal — Selassie was in exile after Italy's invasion
I ask the 52 nations who have given the Ethiopian people a promise to help them: What have you done for Ethiopia? God and history will remember your judgement. It is not merely a question of the settlement of Italian aggression. It is collective security: it is the very existence of the League of Nations.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who is speaking, to whom — and why does the OCCASION matter?
Consider: Selassie had to travel to Geneva personally — his country had been conquered and he was in exile.
WACA Question 2
What specific arguments does Selassie make — and how does he make them persuasive?
Consider: He invokes 'God and history.' He frames it as 'collective security' — not just Ethiopia.
WACA Question 3
How useful is this source for understanding why the League of Nations failed?
Consider: Made AFTER the League had already failed. What does this speech itself tell us about the League's structural weaknesses?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: WACA + Compare with Photograph
  • Apply WACA to this speech fully. Then: how does looking at the photograph change how you understand his words?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is Haile Selassie's 1936 speech for understanding why the League failed?' 150-200 words.
🧠

Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
Winston Churchill, Fulton, Missouri, 5 March 1946
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Truman sat on the platform — Churchill was no longer Prime Minister
From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent. Behind that line lie all the capitals of Central and Eastern Europe — Warsaw, Berlin, Prague, Vienna, Budapest, Belgrade, Bucharest and Sofia.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who gave this speech — and why does Truman's presence on the platform matter?
Consider: Churchill was no longer PM — a private citizen. Yet Truman sat beside him. Does Truman's presence give Churchill's words US government backing?
WACA Question 2
What impression does Churchill give — and how does his language achieve this?
Consider: The phrase 'iron curtain' — why such effective political language? He lists specific European capitals — why name them all?
WACA Question 3
How useful is this source for understanding the origins of the Cold War?
Consider: This speech is both a CAUSE of the Cold War and a DESCRIPTION of it. Can a source be both simultaneously?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: What specific claims does Churchill make?
  • CHECK (WACA): Who? Where/when? Audience (Truman's presence)? Aim?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is Churchill's Iron Curtain speech for understanding why the Cold War began?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
CIA Aerial Photograph, Cuba, 1 November 1962
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: U-2 spy plane photograph — presented to Kennedy and ExComm
[VISUAL SOURCE] An aerial photograph showing a cleared area of Cuban jungle from directly above. Analysts' annotations label specific structures: LAUNCH POSITION, ERECTOR, MISSILE READY TENT, MISSILE TRANSPORTERS. Without these labels, the structures are difficult to identify.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who took this — and why does the CIA source fundamentally affect how we use it?
Consider: This was taken by the CIA during direct confrontation with the USSR. Does a photograph simply record what was there regardless of who took it?
WACA Question 2
What can this photograph show — and what can it NOT show?
Consider: The photograph shows structures. But does it show Soviet INTENTIONS? Who authorised the missiles?
WACA Question 3
How does this photographic evidence relate to Churchill's Iron Curtain speech (1946)?
Consider: Churchill described a Soviet sphere of control in 1946. By 1962, Soviet missiles in Cuba represented a direct military threat.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: Without the labels, what can you actually see? What does this tell us about the role of EXPERT INTERPRETATION?
  • CHECK (WACA): Who took this? When? For whom? CIA's aim?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this CIA aerial photograph for understanding the Cuban Missile Crisis?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
Official Soviet Portrait, c.1930s (state commission)
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Produced during the Great Terror — displayed throughout the USSR
[VISUAL SOURCE] The portrait shows Stalin in a plain military uniform, with a calm and authoritative expression. He appears middle-aged, strong and composed. His gaze is directed slightly upward. There are no other people, no weapons, no signs of violence or terror.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
LOOK: What specific visual choices has the artist made — and what do they communicate?
Consider: List at least FIVE specific visual elements — clothing, expression, pose, lighting, gaze direction, background.
WACA Question 2
CHECK: Who produced this image — and for what audience and purpose?
Consider: This was a STATE COMMISSION. Who was the intended audience? What was the aim? Note: made DURING the Great Terror.
WACA Question 3
JUDGE: How useful is this portrait — and what does the GAP with historical reality tell us?
Consider: This portrait shows none of the Gulag, none of the Holodomor, none of the Show Trials.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: Full LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • Apply LOOK → CHECK → INFER → JUDGE
  • Write your JUDGEMENT: 'This portrait is useful for understanding [specifically what] because..., however the gap between this image and historical reality tells us...'
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this official Soviet portrait for understanding how Stalin maintained power?' 150-200 words.
🧠

Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
